title: Roma Civilization_The History of Rome, Books 27 to 36 by Livy
paragraphs: In Italy, Consul Marcellus regained control of Salapia, which had been surrendered to him. He then captured Maronea and Meles from the Samnites by force. The joy, however, that followed, was by no means so great as the disaster that occurred a few days later, not far from the town of Herdonea. Cneius Fulvius, the consul, was encamped there in the hope of regaining Herdonea, which had revolted from Rome after the defeat at Cannae. His position was neither secure enough due to the nature of the place nor strengthened by guards.

The natural negligence of the general was now increased by the hope that their attachment to the Carthaginians would be shaken when they learned that Hannibal, after the loss of Salapia, had retreated from that neighbourhood into Bruttium. Hannibal ordered his cavalry to ride around as soon as the foot forces were distracted by the battle ahead. He instructed one half of them to attack the enemy's camp, while the other half would strike at their rear, where they were already engaged in combat. The Romans had not been defeated; for after many of them fell in the close contest, and in the engagement with the infantry, they still preserved their ranks and stood their ground. However, the alarm caused by the cavalry on their rear and the enemy's shout, which was heard at the same time from their camp, put the sixth legion to flight. This legion, posted in the second line, was first thrown into confusion by the Numidians. The fifth legion then followed, along with those who were in the van.

Marcellus, undeterred by the magnitude of this disaster, sent a letter to the senate at Rome, detailing the loss of the general and army at Herdonea. He noted that the man who had humbled Hannibal after Cannae's battle, now marched against him, and warned that his present joy would be short-lived. At Rome, the grief occasioned by what had occurred was excessive. The Carthaginians extended their right wing up the hill, with their left wing adjacent to the town. A new and desperate conflict suddenly arose, replacing the one that had been weakly maintained. Their minds and bodies remained unscathed by fatigue. Night intervened, separating the combatants as the outcome of the battle remained uncertain.

During several days, parties of troops sallying from the outposts clashed with both infantry and cavalry in frequent, though largely inconsequential, skirmishes that generally favoured the Romans. Hannibal's army was marched through Apulia without engaging in any notable battles. He moved at night, hoping to set up an ambush, but Marcellus never pursued him, instead choosing to confront him only during the day. After exploring the region, 3 In the meantime, while Flaccus was detained at Capua, selling the property of the nobles and letting out the land that had been forfeited, he rented it all out for rent to be paid in corn. This was done so that there would never be a lack of opportunities to show severity towards the Campanians. A new piece of inquiry that had been developing in secret was now brought to light.

He ordered his soldiers, who had withdrawn from their homes, to build huts in the military style near the gates and walls. This was done immediately so that the houses in the city could be rented out and occupied along with the land. He also did this out of fear that the excessive luxury of the city might weaken his troops, just as it had weakened Hannibal's. The summer was now on its close, and the time for the election of consuls drew near; but a letter from Marcellus, in which he stated that it would not be in the state's interest to depart even one step from Hannibal, whom he was severely pressing while retreating before him and evading an engagement, had excited anxiety. They must either recall the consul from the war at that time when he was most actively employed, or consuls should not be appointed for the year. He had previously sent ambassadors to Spain, to Gnaeus and Publius Cornelius, the Roman generals. Now, he desired to solicit the friendship of the Romans directly, from their own source. The senate sent two ambassadors to the king, along with presents. They were Lucius Genucius, Publius Paetelius, and Publius Popillius.

During the summer in which these transactions took place, prodigies were reported from the countryside and cities in the neighbourhood. At Tusculum, it was said that a lamb had been found with a full stomach of milk; the roof of the temple of Jupiter was struck by lightning and almost stripped of its entire covering. After hostilities had been carried on for nearly sixty years, resulting in significant losses at sea and on land, he had brought the province's operations to a close. As Marcus Valerius Messala arrived on the coast of Africa before dawn, he made a swift descent onto the territory of Utica. He ravaged the area extensively, capturing numerous prisoners and amassing a vast array of plunder. With his spoils in hand, he boarded his ships and set sail for Sicily.

After the dictator arrived in Rome, he appointed Cneius Sempronius Blaesus, who had served as his lieutenant general at Capua, to take command of the Etruscan province. In place of the praetor, Caius Calpurnius, whom he had summoned by letter to lead Capua and his own army, Blaesus was tasked with commanding the army in Etruria. He set the first date possible for the election: which, unfortunately, could not be resolved due to a disagreement between the tribunes and the dictator. They said, "That it was hardly constitutional for a chief magistrate to hold office, but that it was a precedent even more shocking: that the very person who held the election should be appointed."

If the dictator allowed his own name to be included, they would object to the election. However, if the names of others were also on the ballot, they would not interfere. "The dictator defended the election based on the authority of the fathers, the precedent of the commons." The fathers believed that at that time, the state's condition required that its affairs be managed by seasoned and experienced generals, well-versed in the art of war. Consequently, they deemed it essential to expedite the election process. The curule aediles, Lucius Veturius and Publius Licinius Varus, held the Roman games for a single day.

At the close of this year, Caius Laelius, lieutenant general of Scipio, arrived in Rome on the 34th day after departing from Tarraco. Accompanied by a group of captives, he drew a large crowd of people together. The next day, upon entering the Senate, he declared that Carthage, the capital of Spain, had fallen within a single day, that several cities that had rebelled were restored, and that new cities had joined their alliance. I have conquered Carthage in this year, based on the accounts of many writers, despite some claiming it occurred the following year, as it seemed unlikely to me that Scipio would spend a whole year in Spain accomplishing nothing.

Quintus Fabius Maximus entered his office as consul for the fifth time, and Quintus Fulvius Flaccus took up his post for the fourth time. On the same day, Italy was designated as a province under their joint authority. Their commands were subsequently divided into distinct regions. During the election of a chief priest, an old dispute was revived when a priest was appointed to succeed Marcus Aemilius. The patricians denied Caius Mamilius Vitulus, a plebeian candidate, from standing because no one before his time had held that priesthood who was not a patrician. The tribunes, who gave their opinion that justice required it, argued that since the obliteration of the privilege was caused by the negligence of the flamens, the consequences should fall on the flamens themselves, not on the office. This led the flamen into the senate, where he received general approval from the fathers and no opposition, even from the praetor himself. Everyone agreed that the flamen had achieved his goal more due to the purity of his life than any inherent right of the priesthood.

They said they had been drained by levies and contributions for ten years. A Roman citizen who was conscripted by the Romans was more lost to them than one who was captured by the Carthaginians. The latter was returned to their homeland by the enemy without paying a ransom, whereas the former was sent beyond Italy's borders into exile, rather than being forced into military service. They must refuse to the Roman people, before they came to utter desolation and want, what their very condition would refuse.

If the Romans saw their allies unanimous on this point, they would then certainly think of making peace with the Carthaginians. Otherwise, Italy would never be at peace while Hannibal was alive. They discussed their matters in their meetings. The consuls were astonished by this new proceeding and desired to dissuade them from such a hateful measure. Considering it would be more effective to use censure and remonstrance rather than mild means, they said, "They had dared to say to us what we could not bring ourselves to declare in the senate; for this was not refusal to perform military service, but an open defection from the Roman people." They desired, therefore, to return to their colonies quickly, and, assuming the matter was untouched, since they had only discussed it in passing, without taking any action, they decided to consult with their fellow citizens.

Let their names be remembered and praised. While the consuls prepared for battle, all necessary preparations were made. It was then decided to draw upon the vicesimae aureae, a reserve of gold stored in the most sacred part of the treasury, in times of extreme need. It was established at Privernum that an ox spoke, and a vulture flew down into a nearby shop as the forum was crowded.

During the same time, Quintus Fulvius, consul, held an election for the creation of censors. After a long verbal dispute, his colleague gave up the point, and Quintus Fabius Maximus, consul, was chosen by Sempronius, chief of the senate. Another senate was then elected, and eight names were passed over; among them was that of Lucius Caecilius Metellus, who had been disrespected as an adviser to abandoning Italy following his defeat at Cannae.

He urged his colleague in person, and Marcellus by letter, to take the most vigorous measures to detain Hannibal, as he was preparing to make an attack on Tarentum. After executing the order, they did so with great enthusiasm and vigor, attacking the city and putting its cultivators to flight. Marcellus was incited by the consul's letter and, having made up his mind that no Roman general was a better match for Hannibal than himself, set out from his winter quarters as soon as foraging was plentiful in the fields. He then met Hannibal at Canusium.

The country was open, with no cover adapted for an ambush; therefore, he began to retire into woody districts. However, he was drawn into a contest of that kind, which he had been avoiding. While encamping, Marcellus attacked the workmen on all sides, preventing the completion of his works.

The next day, as soon as it was light, Marcellus led out his troops into the field. Hannibal did not decline the challenge but instead exhorted his soldiers at great length, urging them to remember Trasimenus and Cannae, in order to quell the proud spirit of their enemies. "The enemy pressed upon him, and trod upon their heels. He did not allow them to pass unmolested, pitch their camp, or even take a breath and look around. Every day, the rising sun and the Roman troops in battle array could be seen together on the plains." While some retired in confusion, and others came up reluctantly, the entire line was thrown into disarray. Their fears got the better of them, and they were completely routed. In the end, they turned their backs on their shame. Marcellus, upon his return to the camp, delivered a stern address to his soldiers. The severity of his words was palpable, and it was clear that he was still reeling from their defeat. His exasperation was evident, and it only added to the sense of unease among his troops.

These were the same enemies you had pursued throughout the summer, who had fled from you at night and day. You had worn them down with partial battles, and now they refused to march or camp. They advanced into the field. 14 Hannibal, upon receiving intelligence of this, replied, "Surely the enemy we are dealing with cannot tolerate either good or bad fortune."

Hannibal had been uncertain about the outcome of the battle for a long time. He then ordered his elephants to be placed at the forefront, in an attempt to create confusion and panic among the enemy ranks. Marcellus sent his cavalry after them as they fled. They continued their pursuit until they were forced into their camp, driven by fear and consternation. Proceeding thence to Tarentum, he pitched his camp in the very mouth of the harbour. Some of the ships Livius had employed for protecting convoys were loaded with engines and implements for attacking walls, while others were furnished with machines for discharging missiles. The storeships also carried stones and various types of missiles, including those propelled by oars. This allowed some ships to transport engines and ladders to the walls, while others could fire missiles at the defenders from a distance.

As those who attacked Caulon in Bruttium retreated upon the approach of Hannibal, fearing they would be overwhelmed, they took refuge on a nearby hill, safe from an immediate assault. While Fabius was besieging Tarentum, he received assistance in the accomplishment of that great object from a circumstance that, in brief, was insignificant. After the method to be pursued and the time for putting the plan into effect had been agreed upon, a soldier, who was sent out of the city by night, reported to the consul on what had been accomplished and what actions were to be taken.

The consul kept the men silent. Democrates, who had formerly commanded the fleet and happened to be in command in the quarter, saw that all was quiet around him. However, other parts of the city were resounding with a din, as if a captured city's shout was being raised. Fearing loss while he hesitated, the consul should make an attack and advance his standards. He led his party over to the citadel, from which the most alarming noise proceeded. Fabius concluded that the guard had been withdrawn, both from the time elapsed and the silence that prevailed, as not a voice met his ear from a quarter where the noise and bustle of men once resounded. He ordered the ladders to be carried to the part of the wall where the person who had devised the plot for betraying the city had informed him that the Bruttian cohort was on guard.

The wall was first captured in that quarter, with the Bruttians aiding and receiving the Romans; and here they gained entry into the city: after which the nearest gate was broken open to allow the troops to enter en masse. Two of their leaders, Nico and Democrates, fell while fighting bravely. Shortly thereafter, his horse, which had wandered loose through the city, was recognized, but his body could not be found anywhere.

Carthalo, the prefect of the Carthaginian garrison, came unarmed to meet the consul, with the intention of reminding him of a long-standing tradition of hospitality that existed between their families. However, he was suddenly and brutally killed by a soldier who intercepted him. "Thirty thousand slaves were said to have been captured. An immense quantity of silver was found, as well as eighty-three thousand pounds of gold. The statues and pictures were numerous, rivalling those in Syracuse." The wall that separated the city from the citadel was torn down.

While events were unfolding in Tarentum at this time, Hannibal, whose troops had surrendered to the siege of Caulonia, learned of the siege of Tarentum and hastened to provide aid with great urgency, both day and night. However, upon hearing that the city had already fallen, he exclaimed, "Even the Romans have their own Hannibal." We had lost Tarentum by the same means by which we had taken it. He did not appear to be fleeing, so he camped nearby, about five miles from the city. After staying there for a few days, he retired to Metapontum. From this place, he sent two Metapontines with letters from the principal men in the state to Fabius at Tarentum. The message was that they would accept his promise not to punish their past conduct, on condition that they would betray Metapontum along with the Carthaginian garrison into his hands.

After consulting the gods following the sacrifice of a victim, the aruspex warned him to be vigilant against hostile treachery and ambush. After the day set for his arrival had passed without his coming, the Metapontines were sent again to encourage him. However, they arrived too late, as he was already seized by the authorities. The Metapontines' attempt to persuade him was in vain, and they soon realized that their efforts would be met with a harsher treatment if they did not cooperate. 17 In the beginning of summer, when these events took place, Publius Scipio had spent the entire winter in Spain regaining the affections of the barbarians through gifts and by sending back their hostages and prisoners. It was then that Edesco, a prominent Spanish commander, arrived at his side.

Hasdrubal perceived that the strength of the enemy was growing with such large additions, while his own was dwindling, and that events would continue on their present course unless he made a bold effort to alter them. He resolved to engage in battle as soon as possible. Scipio was even more eager for battle, thanks in part to the successes that had attended his operations, which had heightened his hopes. He preferred to engage the enemy alone, with a single general and army, rather than facing them as a unified force. In the event he was compelled to confront multiple armies simultaneously, he employed ingenuity to bolster his forces. Noting that there was no need for ships, as the entire Spanish coast was free from Carthaginian vessels, he beached his ships at Tarraco and incorporated his sailors into his land-based troops. He had an abundance of arms, including those that had been captured at Carthage and those he had commissioned to be made after the city's capture, as a large workforce had been employed.

Indibilis and Mandonius, with their forces, encountered him while on his march. They passed through every place without molestation, and his allies received him courteously, escorting him as he crossed into each district's boundaries. They were conducted to a lodging on that day. The following day, they were received as allies through a treaty. After this, they were sent to build up their forces. They marched through rugged ground, impeded only by the road. Once they reached a distance where arrows could be thrown, an immense quantity of weapons of all kinds was showered upon them. The soldiers, along with a large number of servants, mingled with the troops and threw stones at the enemy, which were scattered throughout the area and proved effective as missiles.

Upon this Seipio, having ordered the victorious troops to mount up and attack the centre of the enemy, he divided his forces with Laelius. He directed Laelius to go round the hill to the right until he found an easier way to ascend, while Seipio himself made a small circuit to the left and charged the enemy in flank. As they turned to face their enemies, their lines became disorganized, and they struggled to reorient themselves in response to the loud shouts that echoed all around them. Laelius also appeared during this confusion, and as the enemy retreated, they created an opening by retreating in disarray, allowing the Roman centre to advance. This was made possible only because the enemy's front line had broken, creating a gap that the Romans could exploit.

As the enemy's forces were being defeated on all fronts, Scipio, leading his left wing in a charge against the enemy's right flank, focused primarily on targeting their exposed side. There were, therefore, approximately seven thousand men slain. 19 After this, a multitude of Spaniards, consisting of those who had surrendered to him before and those whom he had captured the preceding day, gathered around him. They all saluted him as king. When Scipio, after the herald had obtained silence, declared that "in his estimation, the most honourable title was that of general, which his soldiers had conferred upon him."

A few days after the Battle of Baecula, when Scipio returned to Tarraco, he had now cleared the pass of Castulo. The generals, Hasdrubal, son of Gisgo, and Mago, arrived from farther Spain, joining Hasdrubal, who had received late assistance following his defeat. Their arrival was somewhat timely, as it provided counsel on the further prosecution of the war. They then consulted together about the feeling of the Spaniards in the quarters where their various provinces were located. Hasdrubal, son of Gisgo, alone expressed his opinion, that the most remote part of Spain bordering on the ocean and Gades was still unacquainted with the Romans, and might therefore be somewhat friendly to the Carthaginians. That, therefore, though the Carthaginian senate had not decreed it, Hasdrubal must nevertheless march into Italy, the principal seat and object of the war; and thus at the same time lead away all the Spanish soldiers out of Spain.

Marcellus was even under an unfavorable report, not only because he had failed in his first battle, but also because while Hannibal was traveling wherever he pleased throughout Italy, he had led his troops to Venusia in the midst of summer to take up residence. The connections of Marcellus, however, then obtained leave that Marcellus, leaving a lieutenant-general at Venusia, should return to Rome to clear himself of the charges which his enemies were urging, and that the question of depriving him of his command should not be agitated during his absence. The plebeian tribune accused Marcellus and the nobility in general. It was due to their dishonesty and dilatory conduct that Hannibal occupied Italy, as if it were his own province, for nearly ten years; he had spent more of his life there than in Carthage. The Roman people were enjoying the fruits of Marcellus' prolonged command. His army, after being twice defeated, spent the summer at Venusia, lodging in houses. Marcellus so completely destroyed the effect of this harangue by the tribune, who recited the services he had rendered. As a result, not only was the bill to deprive him of his command thrown out, but on the following day, he was unanimously elected consul by all the centuries with great consensus.

It was asserted that Servilius was not qualified to be plebeian tribune or aedile. His father, who had been supposed dead for ten years, was actually alive and in the hands of his enemies. 22 A pestilence attacked the city and the country this year. It manifested primarily through prolonged rather than deadly illnesses. Due to the outbreak, supplication was performed in every street throughout the city. Publius Licinius Varus, the city praetor, was ordered to propose to the people a law that a vow be made to perform these games on a specific day forever.

A letter was written to Caius Hostilius, instructing him not to delay taking hostages from that people. Caius Terentius Varro was then sent, with a command, to receive the hostages and convey them to Rome. After this, the military tribunes, prefects of the allies, and centurions were ordered to keep watch at the gates, so that no one might leave by night. They considered the imminent danger of a commotion in Tuscany. As a result, Caius Terentius was ordered to lead one of the city legions to Arretium and use it to protect the city.

The case of the Tarentines was then warmly debated in the senate. Fabius was present and defended those he had subdued by force of arms. Others felt anger towards them. The majority compared them to the Campanians in terms of guilt and punishment. A decree of the senate was passed in accordance with Manius Acilius' opinion, that the town should be guarded by a garrison, and all Tarentines kept within their walls. Furthermore, it was decided that the question regarding their conduct would be addressed again when the state of Italy became more tranquil. During the Gallic War at Clastidium, he had vowed a temple to Honour and Valour. However, its dedication was hindered by the pontiffs, who argued that it would be improper to dedicate a shrine to two deities. They claimed that if the temple were struck by lightning or any other omen occurred within it, determining which deity should receive sacrifice would be challenging. Furthermore, offering only one victim to both deities would be unlawful unless in exceptional circumstances.

Crispinus, who sought to reduce Locri in Bruttium by siege, believed that the affair of Tarentum had greatly enhanced Fabius' fame. He therefore sent for all types of engines and machines from Sicily; he also sent for ships from the same place to attack the part of the city nearest the sea. He therefore returned from Bruttium into Apulia. The consuls took up positions in two separate camps, located less than three miles apart, between Venusia and Bantia. Hannibal, after diverting the war from Locri, returned to the same region.

He turned his attention entirely to his own unique skills and waited for a suitable moment to set up an ambush. Battles were fought between the two camps, with varying degrees of success. There was a raised ground covered in wood situated between the Punic and Roman camps. It was initially occupied by neither party, as the Romans were unfamiliar with its terrain on that side facing the enemy's camp. In contrast, Hannibal believed it was better suited for an ambush rather than a camp.

Although the consuls found themselves in a precarious position in the valley, where they could neither ascend the hill occupied by the enemy nor escape as they were surrounded on all sides, the contest might have lasted longer had not a retreat initiated by the Tuscans disheartened the remaining troops. The Fregellans, however, refused to surrender, despite being deserted by the Tuscans. The consuls, unscathed, continued the fight by rallying their troops and engaging in combat themselves. The death of Marcellus was an event to be deplored, as it was accompanied by other unfortunate circumstances. Moreover, it was unbecoming of his years, for at sixty-plus, he had acted imprudently, plunging himself, his colleague, and nearly the entire commonwealth into ruin.

He went out of the camp to view the ground, but the accounts vary on other points. They generally agree that he was ambushed. 28 Crispinus, disheartened by the death of his colleague and his own wound, set out under the cover of darkness and camped on the nearest mountain range, choosing a secure location that offered elevation and protection from all directions. The opening was barely wide enough, but the deserters rushed through the gate as soon as it was open. About 600 of them entered before the rope that held it up was released. As the rope went slack, the portcullis dropped with a loud clang.

Some Salapians attacked the deserters, who were carrying their arms carelessly suspended on their shoulders, as is customary after a march, like comrades. Others drove off the enemy by throwing stones, pikes, and javelins from the tower adjacent to the gate and from the walls. As soon as he was informed, by a signal displayed from the watch-towers, that the Numidians were approaching, he suddenly threw open the gate and sallied out boldly upon the enemy. Initially, the contest was doubtful, mainly because his unexpected move had caught them off guard rather than any disparity in strength. However, once the Numidians arrived, the Romans became disheartened, and they fled to the sea and their ships, leaving behind their works and the engines that had battered the walls. It was impossible for him to go to Rome to hold the election, as he feared the exhaustion of the journey and was concerned about Tarentum, fearing that Hannibal might divert his course towards it from Bruttium.

Quintus Fabius the Younger was sent to Venusia with the army, while three consular commissioners were appointed: Sextus Julius Caesar, Lucius Licinius Pollio, and Lucius Cincius Alimentus. This decision came just a few days after he had recently returned from Sicily. Marcus Valerius sailed across the Mediterranean from Sicily into Africa with a fleet of 100 ships. He made landfall near the city of Clupea and ravaged the surrounding countryside, leaving few people armed to resist him. The troops were quickly returned to their ships, and a sudden report informed them that a Carthaginian fleet was approaching.

The Romans fought successfully, not far from the city of Clupea. After defeating the enemy, they took eighteen prisoners and put the rest to flight. They then returned to Lilybaeum, bringing back a significant amount of plunder gained through both land and sea battles. They were harassed by Machanidas, tyrant of the Lacedaemonians, with a war in their immediate neighbourhood. The Aetolians, having passed over an army in ships through the strait between Naupactus and Patrae, called Rhion, had devastated their country. Attalus, king of Asia, was said to intend crossing into Europe because the Aetolians, in their final council, had offered him the position of chief magistrate for their nation. 30

The superintendence of the Heraean and Nemaean games was conferred upon him by the people's suffrages, as the Macedonians' kings traced their origin from that state. After completing the Heraean games, he set out directly for Aegium, to attend the council of allies, which had been fixed beforehand. Proposed measures were put forward to bring an end to the Aetolian War, so that neither the Romans nor Attalus would have a pretext for entering Greece. However, they were all thwarted by the Aetolians before the truce period had barely expired. This happened after they learned that Attalus had arrived at Aegina and that a Roman fleet was stationed at Naupactus. For when called into the council of the Achaeans, where the same embassies were present that had negotiated for peace at Phalara, they initially complained about some minor acts committed during the truce period, contrary to the faith of the convention. However, they ultimately asserted that it was impossible for the war to be terminated unless the Achaeans returned Pylus to the Messenians, unless Atintania was restored to the Romans, and Ardyaea was returned to Scerdilaedus and Pleuratus.

While the king was occupied with the exhibition of the games, he indulged himself in festivity with more freedom than in time of war. Publius Sulpicius, setting out from Naupactus, brought his fleet to the shore between Sicyon and Corinth, and devastated the country without restraint, which was renowned for its fertility. After passing the appointed time for the celebration of the Nemaean games, and a few days more, he set out for Dyme to expel the garrison of the Aetolians, which had been invited by the Eleans and received into the town. Cycliadas, who held the chief direction of affairs, met with the king at Dymae, accompanied by the Achaeans, who were filled with hatred towards the Eleans due to their separation from the rest of the Achaeans and their anger towards the Aetolians, whom they believed had provoked a Roman war against them.

Sulpicius had sailed from Naupactus to Cyllene with 15 ships, landing 4,000 armed men under cover of darkness, so as not to alert his troops. When they encountered the Roman standards and arms among the Aetolians, such an unexpected event caused great terror. Initially, the king wanted to withdraw his troops; however, after a battle broke out between the Aetolians and Trallians, a tribe of Illyrians, he saw his men hard-pressed. The king then led his cavalry in charging a Roman cohort himself. As the contest turned against him, many fell and were wounded around him. His soldiers quickly gathered him up and placed him on another horse, after which he fled the battlefield.

While engaged in distributing spoils and captives, a large force of four thousand men accompanied him, as did twenty thousand head of cattle of various kinds. Intelligence from Macedonia reached him that Eropus had gained control of Lychnidus by bribing the citadel's and garrison's prefect; he also held several towns of the Dassaretians and was attempting to stir up the Dardanians to rebellion. Desisting from the Achaean war, he nonetheless left behind two hundred armed troops under the generals Menippus and Polyphantas to protect his allies. From Dymae, he set out, passing through Achaea, Boeotia, and Euboea, and arrived at Demetrias in Thessaly on the tenth day. 33 The king, having left Achaea, sailed to Aegina with his fleet and met with Attalus.

Titus Quinctius Crispinus, the consul, died at the end of the year, after nominating Titus Manlius Torquatus as dictator to oversee the election and festivities. The Senate ordered the dictator to celebrate the Great Games, a tradition begun by Marcus Aemilius, the city praetor, during the consulship of Caius Flaminius and Cneius Servilius. They then looked for a colleague for him. Considering him a man of the highest talents, they also believed he was of a more forward and vehement disposition than the circumstances of the war or the enemy, Hannibal, required. They resolved it would be right to temper his impetuosity with a cool and prudent colleague.

After eight years following his condemnation, Marcus Claudius Marcellus and Marcus Valerius Laevinus, as consuls, brought him back into the city. However, he reappeared dressed in tattered clothing, with unkempt hair and beard, and displayed a countenance that reflected the profound impact of his disgrace on his appearance. On being heard in the senate on this occasion, after a long interval, he drew the eyes of all upon him. He then gave rise to conversations about the following: "The people have unjustly disgraced an unworthy individual, and it has been detrimental to the state that, in such an important war, they did not benefit from the service and counsel of someone like him." Neither Quintus Fabius nor Marcus Valerius Laevinus could serve alongside Caius Nero, as it was against the law for two patricians to hold public office.

If he had been proven guilty, why should they trust him with another consulate after having entrusted him with the first one improperly. While thus remonstrating and complaining, the fathers rebuked him, putting him in mind that Marcus Furius, who had been recalled from exile, had restored his country when it was at its most vulnerable. Their liberty was at stake, which motivated only the brave, but they also faced the extreme threat of punishment, including an ignominious death. The citadel was also captured during the confusion on one side, where it was thought to be impregnable. 20

Some African deserters, who were among the Roman auxiliaries at that time, observed that while the townsmen were defending those quarters from danger, and the Romans were making approaches to gain access, the most elevated part of the town - protected by a high rock - was neither fortified nor had defenders. The operations of the war continued unabated. The horse, therefore, which had first encountered the enemy, being repulsed, created an alarm among the light-armed troops. The battle would have been fought under the very rampart if not for the legions, whose main strength lay in forming their line, though they had a very short time to do so.

The veterans stood firm against the furious assault, checking the violence of those who followed with the slaughter of the first to reach them. Soon after, the veteran troops themselves made an attempt to charge them. However, seeing that not a single man gave ground, and that they were inflexibly determined on dying where they stood, the veterans extended their line, which the number of their troops enabled them to do with ease. Surrounding their flanks, they slew all who opposed them while fighting in a circle. 23 But these acts were committed by exasperated enemies in the heat of battle, and executed in accordance with the laws of war on men who were armed and fiercely resisting. In another part of the city, a defenseless crowd of women and children was brutally slaughtered by their own countrymen. The bodies of most of them were still alive when they were thrown onto a burning pile, while streams of blood damped the rising flames. Finally, exhausted by the pitiless slaughter of their friends, they threw themselves into the flames, arms and all. When the carnage was now complete, the victorious Romans arrived on the scene. At first sight of such a revolting transaction, they stood in wonder and amazement for some time. However, from a natural rapacity inherent to humanity, they soon wished to seize the gold and silver that glittered among the other materials. Some were caught by the flames, while others were scorched by the hot blasts. The foremost individuals were unable to retreat due to the immense crowd pressing in around them.

After the rest of the people in that quarter, influenced by fear, had submitted to him, Marcius led his victorious troops to Scipio at Carthage. Mago had halted there after his flight. He then gathered a considerable number of ships on the ocean. From the coast of Africa, he collected additional auxiliaries beyond the Strait. Furthermore, with the assistance of Hanno, the prefect from neighboring Spain, he assembled a larger force. After exchanging pledges with the deserters, Marcius and Laelius were sent to the location. The former was accompanied by a light infantry force, while the latter sailed with seven triremes and one quinquereme, with the aim of coordinating their efforts both on land and at sea. 24

Mandonius and Indibilis, dissatisfied with what had occurred, as they had anticipated with certainty that they would have dominion over Spain upon the expulsion of the Carthaginians, called together their countrymen, the Lacetani. They summoned the Celtiberian youth to arms and, in a hostile manner, devastated the territories of the Suessetanians and Sedetanians, who were allies of the Romans. The pay was also demanded with more insolence than was customary or consistent with military subordination. The guards cast reproaches upon the tribunes as they went around to the watch stations. The form of a Roman camp was preserved solely because the tribunes hoped that, catching the spirit of insubordination, they would not be averse from participating in the mutiny and defection. As a result, the tribunes were allowed to dispense justice in their courts, serve as the watch-word, and take turns serving on the outposts and watches. Since they had taken away the power of command, they maintained the appearance of obedience to orders by spontaneously executing their own commands. Afterwards, when they perceived that the tribunes censured and reprobated their proceedings, they attempted to counteract them. Publicly declaring they would not participate in their disorderly conduct, the mutiny took on a distinct character. Following the tribunes' expulsion from their courts and shortly thereafter from the camp, Caius Albius of Cales and Caius Atrius of Umbria were unanimously appointed as commanders, two common soldiers who had been instrumental in sparking the sedition.

Their mistaken belief about Scipio's death had clouded their minds, and they were certain that once the event became public knowledge, all of Spain would soon erupt into war; during this chaos, money could be extracted from allies and neighboring cities plundered; and in this state of turmoil, when anything seemed permissible, their own actions would be less noticeable. 25 After the mutiny was halted, credible individuals brought in reports confirming that Scipio was alive, followed shortly by news that he was in excellent health. As a result, seven military tribunes were dispatched by Scipio himself. A proclamation was issued immediately after this, stating that they were required to attend Carthage to receive their pay, whether they chose to go individually or collectively.

For he had pardoned even his enemies who had fought him with the sword. They realized that their rebellion had not resulted in any casualties or bloodshed, and it was not a serious offense to warrant harsh punishment. They were not far from Carthage when intelligence received from persons on the road informed them that the entire army was marching the following day with Marcus Silanus against the Lacetanians. This news relieved them of all apprehensions they had, though unspoken, and filled them with great joy, for it meant they would have the general alone and in their power, rather than facing him as equals. They refreshed themselves afterwards.

Then all their insolence subsided; and, as they afterwards confessed, nothing terrified them so much as the unexpected vigor and hue of the general, whom they had supposed they should see in a sickly state, and whose countenance was such as they declared they did not remember to have ever seen even in battle. For my own part, I believed that after the Carthaginians were expelled from Spain, there was no place in the whole province where or any person to whom my life was obnoxious. This was due to the manner in which I had conducted myself, both towards my allies and even those who were my enemies.

It was not your intention to hold Sucro as a place of residence. If  While I was alive and the rest of the army was safe, I had taken Carthage in one day. I had routed, put to flight, and expelled four generals and four armies of the Carthaginians from Spain. Did you, who were only eight thousand men, all of course inferior to Albius and Atrius, hope to wrest the province of Spain out of the hands of the Roman people? If so, for what offense on the part of your country? 29 Coriolanus was driven into exile by an unjust sentence of condemnation and a miserable banishment. However, it was his private sense of duty that prevented him from committing the ultimate act of treason – fighting against his own country.

Was the delay in receiving your pay for a few days, during the illness of your general, a sufficient reason for you to declare war against your country? To revolt against the Roman people and join the Ilergetians. To leave no obligation, divine or human, unfulfilled. Let oblivion cover all these things if possible. If not, then let them be covered in silence.

They were tied to the stake, scourged with rods, and beheaded. Those who witnessed the event were paralyzed by fear, rendering them unable to express any form of discontent or even utter a sound. During Hanno's time as lieutenant-general of Mago, he was sent from Gades to the river Baetis with a small group of Africans. He tempted the Spaniards with money, arming around 4,000 men. However, after being deprived of his camp by Lucius Marcius and losing the majority of his troops in the chaos following its capture, some also fled during the pursuit by cavalry as they dispersed. During these transactions on the river Baetis, Laelius sailed out of the straits into the ocean and arrived at Carteia, a city located on the coast of the ocean, where the sea began to expand.

Adherbal, having put the conspirators on board a quinquereme, sent it ahead. He followed himself at a moderate distance with eight triremes. When engaged, one ship would bear down on another, its beak aimed directly at it, taking an oblique position. In this scenario, the two ships collided, with one receiving a blow from the other's beak. Meanwhile, a third ship, exposed to attack, was suddenly turned around to face its pursuers. While the triremes were engaged in a doubtful contest, governed by chance, the Roman quinquereme, either more manageable due to its weight or with more banks of oars, sank two triremes and swept off their oars, one from each side, as it sailed past with great violence.

Laelius returned victorious to Carteia. Upon hearing what had occurred at Gades, he learned that the plot had been discovered, the conspirators were sent to Carthage, and their hopes had been completely frustrated. He then sent a message to Lucius Marcius, instructing him to either return to the general or waste no more time in idle speculation before Gades. Marcius agreed with the proposal, and they both returned to Carthage a few days later. Scipio regained the affection of his soldiers without difficulty, both by punctually discharging all arrears of pay, guilty and innocent alike, and particularly through his conciliatory demeanor towards all. Before leaving Carthage, he summoned an assembly and denounced the perfidy of the petty princes who were in rebellion. He declared that the feelings with which he set out to exact revenge for their treachery were vastly different from those with which he had recently reconciled his own countrymen. He bid them, therefore, to follow him, with the assistance of the gods, not to wage war against an equal foe, but to exact revenge on the most base of men. " 33

After this speech, he dismissed them, with orders to prepare themselves in every respect for marching the next day. When they set out, he arrived at the River Iberus on the tenth day. The Spaniards, as soon as they saw the cattle at a distance, rushed upon them. The light-armed troops then attacked the Spaniards' camp. They initially clashed with missiles, but after firing their lighter arms, designed to stir up tension rather than settle the conflict, they drew their swords and engaged in close combat.

The cavalry's arrival would have been a significant factor, as it charged into battle, trampling all before them. Some also rode around the foot of the hill to attack their rear, aiming to cut off the majority of the infantry; this tactic led to greater carnage than typically seen in light and skirmishing engagements. The valley, which had previously been confined, would not be able to contain all their forces. Scipio conceived that the confined nature of the ground would be in his favour, both because Roman troops were better adapted for fighting in a contracted space than the Spanish, and also because the enemy had come down and formed their line on ground which could not contain all their forces. He applied his mind to a new expedient.

He himself drew up the entire body of his infantry against the enemy, placing four cohorts in front. There were two battles. Two lines of infantry and two bodies of horse were engaged within the space occupied by the plain, lengthwise. It was too narrow to allow both types of forces to be engaged on the same lines. When Spanish infantry could not assist their cavalry, nor could the cavalry support them, the infantry, which had rashly engaged on the plain, relying on the cavalry's assistance, was being cut down. The cavalry, surrounded and unable to withstand the enemy's infantry charge, also fell victim to the same fate. Their own infantry, already defeated, could not provide any further aid. After forming a defensive circle, they fought for a long time, but their horses stood still, and all were killed.

The third part, which stood on a hill to observe the contest from a safe distance rather than engage in it, had ample time and space to flee. The princes, who had managed to escape during the chaos, joined them, having avoided capture before the entire army was encircled. 34 He said that his own condition and that of his brother and the rest of his countrymen was such that either if it seemed good to them, they would give back their lives to him from whom they had received them, or if preserved a second time, they would in return devote their lives forever to serve him alone who was indebted to them for their lives. Scipio stated that Mandonius, who stood before him, and Indibilis, who was absent, had justly forfeited their lives due to their wicked conduct. However, he added that they would be spared because of his own kindness and the Roman people's benevolence.

The negotiation that had been pending for some time began to take shape regarding Masinissa. It was delayed due to various reasons, as the Numidian king was eager to meet Scipio in person and seal their agreement with a handshake. For all that added to his majestic presence was his flowing hair, his simple yet masculine dress, and his youthful vigor, which was restored after a recent illness, giving him an added fullness and sleekness. He had long wished to achieve this, but was unable to do so in Spain, a foreign land. It would have been easy for him to succeed in the country where he was born and educated, given the hope of inheriting his father's throne.

Scipio saw and heard him with great delight, for he knew the young man was the first to join the enemy's cavalry, and the boy's demeanor showed a noble character. He returned to Tarraco after making mutual promises. Mago, who had lost hope for success in Spain, where he had once entertained hopes, received a message from Carthage while preparing to cross into Africa. The senate ordered him to transport his fleet from Gades to Italy. There, he hired as many Gallic and Ligurian youth as possible to join forces with Hannibal, ensuring the war did not falter, which had begun with great vigor and even greater success.

As he sailed along Spain's coast, he landed his troops near New Carthage and after ravaging the surrounding lands, brought his fleet back to the city. The fleet had been spotted during the day, making it clear that choosing a station before the city was advantageous. Many, however, met with a miserable death while attempting to swim to the ships, unable to navigate in the darkness due to their unfamiliarity with the area.

After the fleet had fled back to the ocean from which it had come, a large number of soldiers were killed between the wall and the shore. Two thousand stands of arms were discovered. 37 While they attempted to justify their actions by citing a disorderly assembly of their people, who were enraged against them due to some acts of plunder committed by the soldiers during their departure, he persuaded their suffetes – the chief magistracy among the Carthaginians – along with their quaestor, to attend a conference. A large quantity of stones was poured onto the fleet as it approached the shore. The stones were so thick and heavy that the ships dared not enter the harbor, so they sailed away towards the open sea instead.

They landed here and pitched camp in a strong position above the harbour. With the city and country under their control without a fight, they enlisted 2,000 auxiliary troops, which they sent to Carthage. Then, they hauled their ships ashore for the winter. The people of Gades surrendered to the Romans after Mago's departure from the coast of the ocean. 38 He stated that he had entered Spain against four generals and four victorious armies, but that he had left no Carthaginians in that country.

It is recorded that this election was attended by a greater number of people than any other during the war. In the fourteenth year of the Punic War, Publius Cornelius Scipio and Publius Licinius Crassus entered office as consuls. The provinces assigned to them were Sicily, which was allocated to Scipio without a lottery, as his colleague did not oppose it. This was due to the need for the chief pontiff's presence in Italy to attend to sacred affairs. Bruttium was assigned to Crassus. A Senate was convened in the Capitol. On the motion of Publius Scipio, a decree was passed requiring him to perform the games he had vowed in Spain during the time of the soldier uprising, using funds he had personally contributed to the treasury. 39

He then introduced the Saguntine ambassadors into the senate. The eldest among them spoke: "Although there remains no degree of suffering beyond what we have endured, in order to preserve our faith towards you until the end; yet such are the benefits we have received from yourselves and your generals that we do not regret the calamities we have suffered."

Although there were either no war in Italy or an enemy so formidable that no glory could be gained from conquering him, the man who would retain you in Italy, though motivated by a desire to promote the public good, might appear to wish to deprive you of an opportunity to gain renown when he objected to your leaving the war. Unless Hamilcar is a general more worthy of consideration than Hannibal, or a war in Africa of more importance, or a victory there greater and more glorious, then it would be fitting for us to be victorious as well. You naturally value the fame you've earned more than what you aspire to achieve. However, it's unlikely you would brag more about liberating Spain from war than about liberating Italy.

Attack those of others after defending your own possessions. Let your own house, your father, and your uncle be slain together with their armies within thirty days. After that, having spent several years in performing important services both by sea and land, they had inspired foreign nations with the highest reverence for the Roman people and your family. This should serve as a warning to you. After sailing along the coast of Italy and Gaul to Emporiae, you brought your fleet to land at a city of our allies.

After that, from Tarraco, you marched through places garrisoned by Roman troops. On the banks of the Iberus, your father's and uncle's armies were still more furious after losing their generals, even by the calamity they had suffered. Can you place any confidence in Numidians after having experienced a defection among your own soldiers?

At present, emulation and various causes of dispute between them drive them against each other, because the fear of any foreign enemy is remote. For this reason, I should be less willing, not solely on account of the commonwealth, but also out of personal consideration, that after having been defeated, he should be allowed to march into Italy. Whether here or there, it is with Hannibal that you will have to contend.

For he has so magnified his own honours and the fame of his exploits, in order to do away with the imputation of envy, that it would appear I am in danger of being rivalled by every obscure person. However, not even himself can rival me, for as he enjoys an eminence above everyone else, an eminence to which I also aspire, he is unwilling that I should be placed upon a level with him. He mentions that Marcus Atilius was taken prisoner in Africa. He did not recall that the ports of Africa were open to his commander, despite his misfortune; he had performed some brilliant services in the first year and remained undefeated until the end, particularly against the Carthaginian generals. You will not, therefore, be deterred by that example.

If that disaster had occurred in the present, rather than during the former war, I would still consider it less advisable to travel to Africa after Regulus was captured there, compared to crossing into Spain after the Scipios were defeated there. Why, therefore, since you have leisure to relate Grecian tales, do you not rather set before us the instance of Agathocles, king of Syracuse? He, when Sicily was ravaged by a Punic war for a long time, crossed into Africa and removed the war from its original source. 44 It is unnecessary to resort to ancient and foreign examples to recall the perils of attacking an enemy with terror, while avoiding personal risk and instead putting another in harm's way. Hannibal did not expect that it would come to pass that so many of the states in Italy would rally to his cause, just as they had done after the defeat at Cannae.

I shall have Hannibal as my opponent, but I prefer to pursue him rather than be held here by him. Let Italy, which has been plagued for so long, finally enjoy some peace; let Africa, in turn, be ravaged and destroyed. Scipio was not favored because a rumor had circulated that if he did not secure the senate's approval to make Africa his province, he would present the matter to the people immediately.

"Therefore," he said, "I ask you, tribunes of the people, to support me in refusing to express an opinion. Even if my recommendation were adopted, the consul would not accept it." The consul asserted that it was unfair for the tribunes to intervene, preventing any senator from expressing their opinion in their stead when asked. Having laid the keels of thirty ships, twenty of which were quinqueremes, and ten quadriremes, he prosecuted the work with such diligence that, on the forty-fifth day after the materials were taken from the woods, the ships, being fully equipped and armed, were launched. 46 The Carthaginian, therefore, having deposited his plunder at a nearby town in the Alps, left behind ten warships for its defense.

They therefore ordered Marcus Livius, proconsul, to march his army from Etruria to Ariminum. They also appointed Cneius Servilius in charge, giving him authority to issue orders if necessary for the state's safety, and to deploy city legions under any command he deemed suitable. Scipio, upon his arrival in Sicily, formed his volunteers into cohorts and centuries. If anyone entertained such a feeling, that he would rather they should then confess it to him, than, complaining afterwards, prove themselves idle and useless soldiers to the state.

The Sicilians were responsible for teaching and training them, as the general had decreed that anyone who failed to do so would serve themselves. This body of cavalry proved itself to be admirable, rendering effective service to the state in numerous engagements. He then inspected the legions and selected soldiers who had served the greatest number of campaigns, especially those who had fought alongside Marcellus. He believed they were well-trained and highly skilled in assaulting towns, having been engaged in the siege of Syracuse for a long time. His focus was now on destroying Carthage.

He brought his new ships ashore at Panormus, so they could be kept on land during the winter, as they had been hastily constructed from unseasoned timber. When everything was in readiness for the war, he came to Syracuse, which had hardly yet returned to a state of tranquility, after the violent commotions of the war. A formidable war broke out in Spain that summer, sparked by Indibilis the Hergetian's disdain for the other generals, who were driven by their admiration for Scipio. They had no one else to send to Spain after the Scipios were defeated in Spain, and later, when the Italian war intensified, he was called back to confront Hannibal.

Lentulus concluded that the cavalry could be employed with advantage by those who were first to send them against the enemy's line, which was broken by intervals. He ordered Servius Cornelius, a military tribune, to direct the cavalry to ride at full speed into the spaces left in the enemy's line. The Spaniards, therefore, gave up all thoughts of fighting on horseback and dismounted to fight on foot. When the Roman generals observed that the enemy's ranks were in disarray, filled with trepidation and dismay, as their standards moved back and forth, they urged their men to attack while the enemy was disorganized, and prevent them from forming a new line.

There was an obstinate contest for a considerable time; but those who fought round the king, whose resistance though almost expiring, were afterwards pinned to the earth by a javelin, having at length fallen, overwhelmed with darts. A general flight took place, and the number slain was greater because the horsemen were prevented from remounting, and the Romans pressed impetuously upon the discomfited troops. They did not give over until they had deprived the enemy of their camp. The Spanish who had been defeated in battle or had escaped from it initially scattered throughout the countryside. Later, they returned to their respective homes. 3 Mandonius summoned them to an assembly. After expressing bitter complaints about the losses they had suffered and condemning those responsible for the war, they decided to send ambassadors with proposals to deliver up their arms and make a surrender. These chiefs, laying the blame on Indibilis, the instigator of the war, and the other chiefs who had fallen in the battle, offered to deliver up their arms and surrender themselves. They received for answer that their surrender would be accepted only if they delivered up alive Mandonius and the rest of those who had fomented the war. If they refused, armies would be marched into the territories of the Ilergetians and Ausetanians, and then into those of other states in succession.

To the latter, the Roman commons and Latium provided a steady supply of young men, who grew more vigorous and numerous with each passing year, replacing the many armies that had been destroyed. In contrast, their own people - both those living in the city and those in the countryside - were largely unfit for military service. The Roman army was composed mainly of auxiliaries, who were hired from Africa, a nation known for its untrustworthiness, and whose loyalty shifted with the winds of fortune. Mago neither stirred up any commotion among the Gauls nor joined forces with Hannibal's troops. Meanwhile, Hannibal was experiencing a decline in both his reputation and military strength. 4 Laelius, not Scipio, had arrived with news that his forces were insufficient for a full-scale campaign, but rather suited for raids into the countryside. The main focus of the war remained in Sicily.

While Laelius was employed in carrying off an immense quantity of booty from the country, the inhabitants of which had no arms and were defenseless, Masinissa, moved by the report of the Roman fleet's arrival, came to meet him accompanied by a small body of horsemen. He was driven from his kingdom, but he said he would join him. It was not right for Laelius to remain in Africa, as he believed a fleet had sailed from Carthage, making it unsafe to engage without Scipio's presence. 5

Masinissa departed after this discourse. The ambassador was instructed to gather as many soldiers as feasible. He promptly convened a gathering of the Gauls and Ligurians, as a considerable portion of both tribes were present. He said that he had been sent to help them gain their freedom. As they could see, supplies had been sent from home to support him. However, the outcome depended on whether they would be able to muster a significant force and a large enough army to carry out the war effort.

The purposes of the Ligurians were to be unrestricted, as the Roman troops were far from their lands and cities. It was only fair that they should arm their youth and assume a share in the war effort. After Caius Laelius returned from Africa, Scipio was urged on by Masinissa's exhortations. The soldiers, seeing the spoils from the enemy's country being unloaded from the entire fleet, became eager to cross over as soon as possible. However, this goal was hindered by a minor consideration: regaining the town of Locri, which had defected to the Carthaginians during Italy's general defection at that time. As these nobles were tormented by a longing for their country and inflamed with a desire for revenge against their enemies, they immediately ransomed the prisoners and sent them back after settling on the plan of operation and agreeing upon the signals that would be given from a distance to be observed.

At length, one stirred another, and the fact became more certain. Now every man shouted "To arms" with all his might. The enemy was in the citadel, and the sentinels were slain. However, the Romans, who were far inferior in numbers, would have been overpowered had not a shout from those outside the citadel rendered it uncertain whence the noise came. The Carthaginians, therefore, terrified, supposed the citadel was already filled with the enemy, and thus abandoned all thought of resistance, fleeing instead to the neighboring citadel. Hannibal also sent a messenger in advance from the river Butrotus, not far from Locri's town, to urge his party to attack the Romans and Locrians at dawn in the most vigorous manner. Meanwhile, he launched a simultaneous assault on the town, which would be unprepared for such a measure, as every one would have their attention occupied with the tumult created in the other quarter.

Having thrown together his baggage in a heap, he displayed his line at a distance from the walls to intimidate the enemy, while preparations were being made for an assault. He then rode around the city with some Numidian horsemen to observe where the attack should be launched most effectively. Those who remained in the citadel, after setting fire to the buildings they occupied, fled quickly to avoid raising an alarm. Their goal was to delay their enemy's pursuit, allowing them to escape with their lives. 8 He said that regarding the Locrians as a whole, he would neither give them anything nor take anything from them.

He was confident about one thing: despite having earned a bad fate at the hands of the Romans, he believed they would fare better under Roman rule, even if they were angry with them, than they had when in the control of their former allies, the Carthaginians. The most shocking insults were committed against their own persons, children, and wives. Their rapacity did not abstain from desecrating even sacred things; other temples were violated, but also the treasures of Proserpina, which had never been touched through all ages, except that they were said to have been carried away by Pyrrhus, who restored the spoils along with a costly offering in expiation for his sacrilege. Therefore, as on a previous occasion, the royal ships, wrecked and shattered, brought nothing safe to land, except for the sacred money of the goddess, which they were carrying away. Similarly, now, that same money, by a different kind of calamity, cast a spirit of madness upon all who came into contact with this desecration of the temple, turning them against each other with the ferocity of enemies, general against general, and soldier against soldier. 9

As one of Plinius's men ran away with a stolen silver cup from a townhouse, pursued by its owner, he unexpectedly met Sergius and Matienus, two military tribunes. When the soldiers of Pleminius, who had been defeated, rushed to him in crowds, accompanied by loud clamouring and indignant feelings, they displayed their blood and wounds, and reiterated the reproaches that had been leveled against him during the dispute. Enraged, Pleminius threw himself out of his house, ordered the tribunes to be summoned, and then stripped them bare, producing the rods. Scipio, who had arrived at Messana a few days earlier, passed through Locri on a ship with six rows of oars, learned about the reasons behind Pleminius' actions and those of the tribunes.

Pleminius, unable to restrain his resentment, thought that the injury he had sustained had been treated negligently by Scipio. He believed no one could estimate the punishment that should be inflicted unless they had felt its atrocity personally. Ordering the tribunes to be dragged before him, Pleminius subjected them to every form of torture and then put them to death. His cruelty did not end there; he also cast out their bodies unburied. They began to consider a method for transporting the goddess to Rome, so that they might quickly seize victory, foretold by fate, omens, and prophecies. 11

The answer given was that they would obtain what they were seeking through King Attalus. They brought the goddess to Rome and ensured that the best man in Rome received her with his hospitality. The ambassadors arrived at Pergamum, where the king welcomed them graciously. He then escorted them to Pessinus in Phrygia and presented them with a sacred stone, claiming it was the mother of the gods. The king instructed them to deliver this stone to Rome. Cneius and Lucius Cornelius Lentulus were the curule aediles. Lucius held the province of Spain. He was elected in his absence and served while away from Rome.

Marcus Marcellus dedicated the temple of Virtue at the Porta Capena this year, seventeen years after it was vowed by his father during his first consulate at Clastidium in Gaul. Marcus Aemilius Regillus, flamen of Mars, also died this year. 12 Had he not exerted himself to the utmost in expediting this measure, he would have been overpowered by Publius Sempronius, the proconsul sent to succeed Sulpicius, while engaged in war with the Aetolians. He was aided by ten thousand infantry and a thousand horse, as well as thirty-five ships of war, a significant force that brought substantial assistance to allies. During the same time, the Epirots, wearied by the long continuance of the war, first sounded the disposition of the Romans and sent ambassadors to Philip on the subject of a common peace. They affirmed that they were well satisfied that it might be arranged if he would come to a conference with Publius Sempronius, the Roman general.

Philip, the ruler of Phoenice in Epirus, consulted with Aeropus Dardas and Publius Sempronius. All the tribes agreed on ordering it, as the operations of the war had been transferred to Africa. They were eager to be relieved from all other conflicts in the present. Marcus Livius and Spurius Lucretius were also in command, along with two legions that had protected Gaul against Mago. Cneius Octavius was also present, with orders to deliver up Sardinia and a legion to Titus Claudius after he had surrendered, and was to protect the sea-coast within limits set by the senate, accompanied by forty ships of war.

Although Africa had not yet been openly declared a province, the senate kept it a secret. It is likely they did so to prevent the Carthaginians from gaining intelligence of this development beforehand. Nevertheless, the city held out hope that the enemy would be defeated in Africa that year, and that the Punic War was nearing its end. The matrons, passing her from one to another in orderly succession, carried the goddess into the temple of Victory, located in the Palatium, on the day before the Ides of April, which was declared a festival. As the whole city poured out to meet her, they placed censers at their doors along the route she would take in procession. Kindling frankincense, they prayed that she would enter Rome willingly and propitiously. The entire public's anxiety was focused on King Antiochus, particularly after the arrival of the ten ambassadors. " 45

The senate, despite receiving intelligence from men of great authority who had also investigated all reported matters, deemed the business concerning Antiochus less pressing. Hannibal was elected praetor while they were in possession of this despotism. He summoned the quaestor before him. The quaestor disregarded the summons because he belonged to the opposing faction. Furthermore, as was customary practice, after assuming the quaestorship, individuals were typically promoted to the order of judges, making him well-suited for the powerful position he would soon hold. He also added a new act, one that benefited the people but sparked personal animosity towards him.

A strenuous opposition was made by Scipio Africanus for a long time, however. He thought it highly unbecoming of the Roman people to take sides in the animosities and charges against Hannibal; instead, he preferred to maintain public authority above factional divisions among the Carthaginians, even after having conquered that commander in the field. He therefore determined to give way to fortune and the times. Having already made every necessary preparation for flight, he appeared in the forum that day to avoid suspicion. As soon as it grew dark, he left, dressed in common attire, accompanied by his two attendants, who were unaware of his intention. 48 He sailed over the same day to the island of Cercina, where he found several Phoenician merchant ships with their cargoes. On landing, he was surrounded by a crowd of people who came to pay their respects. In response to any inquiries, he gave orders that it should be said he had been sent as an ambassador to Tyre. Fearing that some of these ships might sail under cover of night to Thapsus or Adrumetum, and carry news of his being seen at Cercina, he ordered a sacrifice to be prepared. The masters of the ships, along with the merchants, were invited to join in the festivities. The sails and yards from the ships were collected and used to create a canopy on shore for the company's supper, as it was the middle of summer.

Hannibal, after a successful voyage, arrived at Tyre. He was received by the founders of Carthage, who treated him like a man from his own homeland. For a few days, he stayed in Tyre. He then sailed to Antioch. Hearing that the king had already departed, he arranged for a meeting with his son, who was celebrating the games at Daphne. The son received him warmly and treated him kindly. After their meeting, he set sail immediately. I should like to hear about this important matter that has prompted the matrons to venture out into public in such an excited state, barely containing themselves from rushing into the forum and the gathering of the people. He has used terms like cabal and sedition; and sometimes referred to secession by women.
