At last, the decemvirs were ordered to consult the books; and, in pursuance of their answer, a supplication was performed during three days. Then the consuls first, and afterwards the praetors, cast lots for their provinces. At the head of the embassy were Menippus and Hegesianax. The former of whom said, "I do not conceive what intricacy there is in the business of our embassy, as we come simply to ask for friendship and conclude an alliance."

There were three kinds of treaties formed by kings and states to establish friendships with each other. One type was when terms were imposed on a people who had been defeated in war. After their possessions had been surrendered to the victorious leader, he had the power to decide how much of it the defeated party would receive and what they would be denied. As Antiochus came under this last description, he wondered that it was becoming for the Romans to dictate terms to him. He asked which city in Asia should be free and independent, which should be subject to tribute, and which of them the king's troops and the king himself were prohibited from entering. Since Antiochus thinks it conducive to his glory, he intends to enslave those cities that were held by his great-grandfather through arms, but which neither his grandfather nor father ever occupied as their own. The Roman people, having taken up the patronage of Greek liberty, believe it is their duty to uphold this faith and constancy, lest they abandon it.

When Hegesianax hesitated and could not deny that the cause of liberty carried a more honorable semblance than that of slavery, Publius Sulpicius, the eldest of the ten ambassadors, replied, "Let us cut to the chase." Hannibal, having left his own country, went to Antiochus, as mentioned earlier. The king held him in high esteem, not just because of his other qualities, but primarily because Hannibal was an ideal candidate to participate in the king's plans for war with Rome. The subject was first discussed among their circles and at their gatherings; eventually, some individuals declared in the senate that "the banishment of Hannibal had no effect if, while residing in another country, he could still devise plans to alter the administration and disrupt the state's tranquility through his machinations."

A warm debate ensued. Some earnestly pressed that he should be immediately seized as a spy and kept in custody, while others insisted there were not sufficient grounds for such violent measures. They argued that putting strangers into confinement without reason was a step that set a bad precedent. This concern was echoed by the Carthaginians at Tyre and other markets where they frequently traded. The question was adjourned. The imputation being thus thrown on the state, less pains were taken in searching into the suspicions harbored by a few individuals. However, it was determined that ambassadors should be sent to Rome to represent the affair to the consuls and the senate, and at the same time to complain of the injuries received from Masinissa. 62 They accordingly sent Publius Scipio Africanus, Caius Cornelius Cethegus, and Marcus Minucius Rufus. After viewing the ground and hearing what could be said on both sides, they left everything in suspense, their opinions remaining undecided.

Whether they acted in this manner out of their own judgment or at the behest of others is far from certain. However, it is clear that leaving the dispute unresolved was the most prudent course of action, given the circumstances. If the situation had been different, Scipio alone could have easily resolved the issue, either through his own knowledge of the matter or due to his influence over both parties, thereby ending the controversy with a mere nod. In the same year, Sextus Digitius, a praetor in Hither Spain, clashed with those states, which, after Marcus Cato's departure, resumed hostilities. The numerous battles were unremarkable, and all unfavorable to him. He barely managed to leave half his forces for his successor. Afterwards, when propraetor, as the Lusitanians, had ravaged the farther Province and were returning home with an immense booty, he attacked them on their march. The engagement lasted from the third hour of the day to the eighth, with neither side gaining any advantage before that point.

He said, "With an army like that, for very little confidence could be placed in the troops of Sextus Digitius, he would conduct the war." "The elder part of the senate insisted that decrees of the senate should not be passed based on rumors fabricated by private individuals for the benefit of magistrates. No intelligence was considered authentic unless it was either written by the praetors themselves in their provinces or brought to them by their deputies." He did not think it prudent to hazard a general engagement, as his troops were raw and composed of many different kinds of men. They were not yet well known among themselves, which made it difficult for them to rely on one another. While operations remained stagnant at Pisae, Lucius Cornelius Merula led his army across the extreme borders of the Ligurians into the territory of the Boians, where the method of proceeding differed significantly from that of the war against Liguria. The consul led the charge; the enemy refused to engage. When no one else would confront them, the Romans took matters into their own hands and launched a surprise attack, while the Boians preferred to allow their homeland to be ravaged without defending it.

He ordered the two military tribunes, Quintus Minucius and Publius Minucius, to lead off the cavalry onto open ground, at a safe distance from the line. When he gave them the signal, they were to charge the enemy through the clear space. While he was thus employed, a message arrived from Tiberius Sempronius Longus, stating that the Extraordinaries could not withstand the Gauls' onset. Great numbers had already fallen; and partly due to weariness, partly due to fear, the ardour of the survivors had greatly diminished. He recommended that the consul consider sending either of the two legions before the army suffered disgrace. The consul urged his soldiers to continue their efforts a little longer, for victory was within their grasp. He ordered them to press the enemy, while they saw them disordered and dismayed. This would prevent the enemy from regrouping and launching another attack with uncertain success. He ordered the standard-bearers to advance with the standards, and then, all exerting themselves at once, they finally forced the enemy to yield.

He therefore advised that, if the Senate thought it proper, they should instruct his colleague, who had already won his war, to return to Rome for the upcoming elections. Another concern that was also pressed upon them was that the public was heavily distressed by usurious practices. Although avarice had been restricted by many laws respecting usury, a fraudulent course had been adopted: transferring securities to subjects of some allied states who were not bound by those laws. This allowed usurers to overwhelm their debtors with unlimited interest. The senate decreed that a certain day should be fixed for putting an end to this evil. The next approaching festival of the infernal deities would serve as that day. Any allies who lent money to Roman citizens from that point forward were required to register the transaction. All subsequent proceedings respecting such loans would be governed by the laws of either state, whichever the debtor chose. In some time after, when a great amount of debt contracted through this kind of fraud was discovered by means of the registries, Marcus Sempronius, plebeian tribune, proposed to the people. The senate directed him, and the people ordered that the laws relative to money lent between Roman citizens and subjects of allied states or Latin confederacy should be the same as those between Roman citizens.

Several engagements took place during the winter, but none were worthy of mention, as they were directed against incursions by robbers rather than enemy forces; nonetheless, with varying degrees of success, and at a cost of some men's lives. Meanwhile, in Spain, the day of election was approaching. Lucius Cornelius, the consul, left Marcus Claudius, lieutenant-general, in command of the army and returned to Rome.

After representing in the senate the services he had performed and the present state of the province, he expostulated with the conscript fathers on their not having ordered a thanksgiving to the immortal gods when such a great war was happily terminated by one successful battle. He then demanded that they would at the same time decree a supplication and a triumph. Before the question was put, Quintus Metellus, who had served as consul and dictator, stated that letters had arrived simultaneously from Lucius Cornelius, the consul, to the senate, and from Marcus Marcellus to a significant portion of the senators. These letters contradicted each other, prompting the consideration of the business to be postponed until the writers could present themselves. He had expected that the consul, aware that the lieutenant-general had written something unfavorable to his advantage, would bring him along when he himself was required to attend, especially since command of the army would be more fitting for Tiberius Sempronius, who already held authority.

He held that the Senate should not approve either of the decrees requested by the Consul. "When he persisted with undiminished energy in putting forward the question of ordering a thanksgiving and allowing himself to ride into the city in triumph, the plebeian tribunes Marcus and Caius Titinius declared that they would enter their protest if the Senate passed any decree on the matter." 9 Marcus Porcius Cato dedicated a chapel to Maiden Victory, near the temple of Victory, two years after his vow. A Latin colony was established in the Thurian territory this year by commissioners appointed for the purpose. They were Cneius Manlius Vulso, Lucius Apustius Fullo, and Quintus Aelius Tubero, who had proposed the order for its settlement.

Twenty-five acres were allocated to each footman, and fifty to a horseman. However, following Apustius' counsel, one-third of the land was set aside for future use, allowing them to establish a new colony at a later time when they deemed it suitable. He had served as consul for the second time after the final defeat of Hannibal, and was also censor. All Quinctius's claims to public favor were fresh and new; since his triumph, he had neither asked nor received anything from the people. "He solicited," he said, "in favor of his own brother, not a half-brother; in favor of his lieutenant-general, and partner in the administration of the war. His brother had conducted operations by sea, while he did the same on land." "He had made his case."

His brother was preferred over the brother of Africanus, despite being supported by the entire Cornelian family. One member of the same family presided at the election, and although the senate gave him a highly honourable testimony in his favour, declaring him the best man in the state, he was still appointed to receive the Idaean Mother into the city when she arrived from Pessinus. For a long time, nothing worth recording had occurred in Liguria. However, towards the end of this year, Roman affairs there were brought into great peril twice. The consul's camp, under attack, was preserved with difficulty. Shortly after, as the Roman army marched through a defile, the Ligurians seized on the opening they intended to pass through. They then set fire to the nearest village, razing it to ashes as they swept through the surrounding area with fire and steel.

Neither the Boians nor the Spaniards, with whom they had been at war during that year, were as bitter and inveterate foes to the Romans as the nation of the Aetolians. After the Roman armies left Greece, these cities held out hope that Antiochus would conquer Europe without resistance. Neither Philip nor Nabis would be able to remain at peace. After Thoas, their praetor, complained about the Romans' injurious behavior and the current state of Aetolia, he argued that "of all the nations and states of Greece, they had been most dishonored after the victory they themselves had achieved." Moved by this, he proposed sending ambassadors to each king, not only to gauge their dispositions but also to persuade them to go to war with Rome.

He was now pent up almost within the walls of his capital, while he saw the Achaeans dominating over the whole Peloponnesus. In addition to this, he introduced the ancient renown of the Macedonian kings, and the whole world was pervaded by the victorious marches of that nation. "The plan which he proposed," he said, "was free from any danger, either in its commencement or in its issue."

The charge of protecting all Lacedaemonians on the coast had been entrusted to Titus Quinctius by the Achaeans. They immediately sent ambassadors to the tyrant to remind him of his treaty with Rome and to warn him against violating a peace he had so earnestly sought. King Antiochus, having solemnized the nuptials of his daughter with Ptolemy, king of Egypt, at Raphia in Phoenicia, returned to Antioch and then traveled through Cilicia, passing over Mount Taurus, to arrive at the city of Ephesus. He sent his son Antiochus into Syria in the spring to guard the remote frontiers of his dominions. If any disturbance arose during his absence, he would be informed. Then, he marched with all his land forces to attack the Pisidians who lived near Sidon.

Therefore, with all his influence, he urged the Romans to go to war. 14 Villius, upon hearing that the king was waging war in Pisidia, proceeded to Ephesus. During a brief stay in the city, he made efforts to secure regular meetings with Hannibal, who happened to be present at the time. This allowed Villius to gauge Hannibal's intentions and alleviate his concerns about potential danger from the Romans. The king wished to avoid further debate with the envoys. He had found no advantage in the former conference or was too discomposed by recent grief. To resolve this, Minio spoke on behalf of the king and convinced him to summon the ambassadors from Pergamum for a new meeting. 16

Sulpicius had recovered his health by this time. He was accompanied by Villius, and together they arrived in Ephesus. Minio then delivered a well-rehearsed speech, saying, "I have observed, Romans, that you claim to hold noble intentions, particularly in regards to liberating the Greek states; however, your actions fail to align with these claims." For if the circumstance of their having once been subject to a foreigner, when crushed under the severity of times, conveys a right to enforce that subjection again after many generations, what can be said of our delivering Greece from Philip, but that nothing was accomplished by us? His successors may reclaim Corinth, Chalcis, Demetrias, and the whole nation of Thessaly.

They introduced many new ideas, and although each one expressed their own grievances and occasionally made demands, some of these ideas were reasonable while others were not. This led to a shift in the discussion from a thoughtful debate to a heated argument. The ambassadors, therefore, without conceding or carrying any one point, returned to Rome exactly as they had come, leaving everything in an undecided state. In the extremities of Greece, Nabis, on the side of Peloponnesus, set everything in motion to recapture Argos and its maritime cities, which he had been expelled from by the Romans and was now confined within Lacedaemon's walls. Meanwhile, Philip on the side of Macedonia would be prepared for battle as soon as he heard the alarm sounded.

He knew his spirit and his temper. He knew that, like wild beasts confined by bars or chains, he had spent a long time harboring the fiercest resentments in his chest. He recalled how often, during the war, the prince had prayed to all the gods to grant him Antiochus as an ally. If this prayer was now answered favorably, he would not hesitate for a moment to take up his arms again. He initially endured this affront in silence, but later decided it was prudent to seize a suitable moment to investigate the cause of the king's sudden withdrawal of favor and to exonerate himself from any blame.

He inquired about the king's displeasure without any introduction. Upon hearing it, he replied, "Antiochus, when I was still an infant, my father Hamilcar, at the time of his sacrifice, brought me before the altar and swore that I would never befriend the Roman people." If, therefore, your courtiers have conceived the idea of ingratiating themselves with you by suggesting suspicions about me, let them seek a way to advance their reputation that does not involve harming me. When planning to wage war against Rome, consider Hannibal as one of your strongest allies.

To the said consul, permission was granted to raise two new legions and 20,000 infantrymen, accompanied by 900 cavalrymen, among the allies and Latin confederates. The provinces of the two last were changed first by a decree of the Senate, which was later confirmed by an order of the people. It was said that a boy was born in Arretium who had but one hand. A shower of earth fell at Amiternum. A gate and wall at Formiae were struck by lightning. What was more alarming than all was when an ox belonging to the consul Cneius Domitius spoke these words: "Rome, take care of yourself." To atone for the other prodigies, a supplication was performed. The ox was ordered by the augurs to be carefully preserved and fed.

During the night, the Ligurians stole away unnoticed; and at dawn, the Romans took possession of their deserted camp, where the amount of plunder found was less because the enemy frequently sent home the spoils taken in the countryside. Minucius, after this, allowed them no rest. In both Spain, Roman arms enjoyed success during that year.

Caius Flaminius, after a siege, captured Litabrum, a strong and opulent city, making Corribilo, a powerful chieftain, his prisoner. Meanwhile, Marcus Fulvius, the proconsul, won two successful battles against two opposing armies of the enemy. While besieging this place, a large army of Vectonians arrived to relieve the Toletans, but he defeated them in a general engagement, and after defeating the Vectonians, captured Toletum. 23 Although they had made careful inquiries through their ambassadors at times, rumors that were spread without a solid basis often mixed false information with the truth.

The senate, therefore, though they had already dispatched the praetor Atilius with a squadron to Greece, yet considered it necessary to send ambassadors of equal quality to secure the attachment of the allies. They sent Titus Quinctius, Caius Octavius, Cneius Servilius, and Publius Villius to Greece at the same time. In their decree, they ordered Marcus Baebius to lead his legions from Bruttium to Tarentum and Brundusium, so that if occasion required, he could transport them into Macedonia. As more messengers arrived with news that war was imminent, it became clear that electing consuls was essential. While the new magistrates were settling the distribution of their provinces, orders were sent to Marcus Baebius to pass over with all his forces from Brundisium to Epirus, and to keep the army stationed near Apollonia. Meanwhile, Marcus Fulvius, city praetor, was commissioned to build a fleet of new quinqueremes. 25

While some of the principal members persisted in their initial opinion, others argued that they should follow the counsel of the person to whom they had applied for advice. The majority waited to hear the sentiments of Philopoemen. The war was therefore unanimously decided upon: the time and manner of conducting it were left to the praetor without restriction. While his quarters were here, and very few of his men had tents, the generality of them having formed huts of reeds interwoven with leaves of trees to serve merely as shelter, Philopoemen resolved before coming into sight to surprise him by an attack of a kind he did not expect. He landed here and made his way by night through familiar paths to Pleiae. As the sentinels slept, unaware of any immediate danger, he set fire to the huts throughout the camp.

A small group managed to escape from both methods of destruction and fled to the main camp near Gythium. Nabis, after capturing Gythium, set out with a force of light infantry and marched rapidly through Lacedaemon. He then seized control of a location known as the Camp of Pyrrhus, where he was confident the Achaeans would establish their presence. The line was closed by the cavalry and the majority of the auxiliaries, as Philopoemen anticipated that the tyrant would launch a surprise attack from behind, relying on his mercenary troops, whom he considered most trustworthy.

Philopoemen was possessed of an admirable degree of skill and experience in conducting a march and choosing his position. He had made this his principal study, both in times of war and during periods of peace. They would have to fight the next day at the river in support of the water parties. During the night, Philopoemen concealed a large number of archers in a remote valley out of sight of the enemy. 29

The fight was doubtful for a considerable time as troops on both sides were of the same kind and armed alike. However, as the contest advanced, the tyrant's auxiliaries gained an advantage due to their superior numbers and because Philopoemen had instructed his officers to retreat after a short period, luring the enemy into the ambush site. Although the deserter did not gain entire credit, but he gave one person a plausible reason for leaving their camp. As their adversaries drew near, seeing that their friends had abandoned them, the Trojans initially tried to retreat into their own works. However, when the entire Achaean force advanced in battle formation, they became filled with fear, fearing that they might also be captured along with their camp. Consequently, they decided to follow the body of their army, which by now had moved a considerable distance ahead. Philopoemen ordered the auxiliaries to press on whenever possible, in pursuit of the enemy. The horsemen found it especially challenging to continue their flight. Meanwhile, Philopoemen led his heavy infantry through more open terrain towards the River Eurotas.

As the tyrant was now confined within the city, Philopoemen spent the greatest part of thirty succeeding days ravaging the lands of the Lacedaemonians. He then greatly reduced and almost annihilated the strength of the tyrant before returning home. The Achaeans extolled him as equal in the glory of his services to the Roman general, and even considered him superior in their war with Lacedaemon. 31 The Achaeans and the tyrant continued their war efforts. Meanwhile, Roman ambassadors traveled through the allied cities, concerned that the Aetolians might persuade some of them to support Antiochus. They took little pains in their dealings with the Achaeans, as they knew of their strong opposition to Nabis and thus felt secure in relying on them for other matters.

They traveled to Athens, then to Chalcis, and finally to Thessaly. After addressing the Thessalians in a full assembly, they decided to head to Demetrias, where a council of the Magnetians had been convened. There was more studied address required to be delivered; for many of the leading men were disaffected towards the Romans and entirely devoted to the interests of Antiochus and the Aetolians. This was because, at the time when accounts were received that Philip's son, who was a hostage, would be restored to him, and the tribute imposed on him remitted, there were also groundless reports circulating that the Romans intended to restore Demetrius to him as well. Every man was answerable for his own absurdities.

Before the council met, two men filled everyone's ears with boasts about the naval and land forces approaching. "A vast army," they said, "of horse and foot is marching from India." They also claimed to be bringing a large quantity of gold and silver, enough to buy even the Romans themselves. Although the matter had almost come to a head, Quinctius thought it advisable to have some ambassadors of the allies present at the council. They could serve as a reminder of Rome's alliance with Aetolia and provide the courage to express their opinions freely, even in opposition to the king's ambassador. Quinctius requested that they send ambassadors to the Panaetolic Council.

After Menippus, he introduced the idea that "it would have been best for all Greeks living in Greece and Asia if Antiochus could have participated in their affairs while Philip's power was still unbroken. In this scenario, everyone would have received what was rightfully theirs, and the entire region would not have fallen under Roman control." Even as matters stand at present," he said, "provided you have the constancy to carry out the measures you've adopted, Antiochus will be able, with divine assistance and Aetolian alliance, to restore Greece's affairs to their former dignity, despite its current low state." The Aetolians, who sought to appear as if they intended to follow Antiochus every step, without taking any direct action themselves, sat inactive and waited for the king's arrival. However, after the Romans departed, although they did not convene a council of the entire nation, they still attempted to devise schemes through their Apocleti – a more discreet gathering of selected individuals – to stir up Greece.

The last was assisted by the exile Eurylochus, whose flight and its cause had been mentioned earlier, as there was no other hope for his return to his homeland. Having marched an extraordinary length of way during that day and the following night, he arrived within six miles of the city at the first dawn. He then chose three troops and led them forward, ordering the rest of the cavalry to follow. Although he had lost control of the maritime towns to the Romans, and was subsequently confined within the walls of his city by the Achaeans, they believed that anyone who seized the chance to kill him would earn the gratitude of all the Lacedaemonians.

Alexamenus made it his custom to ride alongside Nabis through a few of the ranks, offering such advice as seemed most suitable. He would then join his own troops in the right wing and, after giving any necessary orders, return to the tyrant. But on the day he had set for the deed of death, after accompanying the tyrant for a little while, he withdrew to his own soldiers and addressed the horsemen sent from home in these words: "Young men, now is the time to dare and do that deed which you were ordered to execute valiantly under my guidance." The horsemen aimed their lances at him as he lay. After several ineffective strikes against his armor, their tips eventually pierced his body. Before aid could reach him from the center, he had died. 36

The insolence of their behaviour and contempt for their numbers gave the Lacedaemonians courage to assemble in a body. Some argued that they should drive out the Aetolians and resume their liberty, which had been taken from them at the time it seemed to be restored. Others suggested that, for appearances' sake, they should associate with the Aetolians one of the royal family as their leader. Aulus Atilius arrived at Gythium with a fleet of 24 ships. 37

Meanwhile, Thoas failed in his attempt on Chalcis, unlike Eurylochus, who successfully captured Demetrias. This was largely due to the intervention of Euthymidas, a man of significant influence, who had been banished by those loyal to the Roman alliance after Titus Quinctius and the ambassadors arrived. Additionally, Herodorus, a wealthy merchant from Cios, also played a role in Thoas's failure, as his wealth granted him considerable power at Chalcis. Mictio and Xenoclides, now in possession of supreme power since Euthymidas' banishment, suspected nothing or received information about it. Initially, they were so terrified that they saw no safety but flight; however, after their fear subsided, they realized that fleeing would betray not only their country but also the Roman alliance. They then applied their minds to a plan. Both states, therefore, were armed and sent the main strength of their young men.

They dispatched a herald and then ambassadors from that place to inquire among the Aetolians why their friends and allies had come to invade them. Herodorus, after waiting several days at Atalanta, watched anxiously for a signal in vain. He then sent an advice-boat to learn the cause of the delay. Realizing that his associates had abandoned the enterprise, Herodorus returned to Thronium, where he had originally come from. 39 Villius, unable to complete any part of his business, returned to Quinctius. Quinctius then dispatched orders to the Thessalian praetor, instructing him to lead Villius' troops back to Corinth, while Villius sailed his ships there as well. 40

The events in Greece and their connection to Rome have diverted me from my path. They were not inherently noteworthy, but rather served as the catalyst for the conflict with Antiochus. After the consulship, for which I digressed, the consuls, Lucius Quinctius and Gnaeus Domitius, repaired to their provinces. Quinctius went to Liguria, while Domitius marched against the Boii. An earthquake lasted for 38 days. During this time, there was a total cessation of business, accompanied by anxiety and fear.

The year was now almost at an end, while rumors of impending hostility and the anxiety of the senate daily increased. Consequently, they set about adjusting the provinces of the magistrates elected to ensure they were more intent on duty. Two temples were dedicated this year to Jupiter in the Capitol: one had been vowed by Lucius Furius Purpureo when he was praetor during the Gallic war; the other by him again when he was consul.

The Romans were busy preparing for a new war. Antiochus, however, was far from idle. People who have lower mental abilities due to their social status are often envious, as they resent not only someone else's achievements but also their wealth. He first touched down on the island of Imbrus; then he sailed over to Sciathus. From there, after reuniting with the ships that had been separated during the voyage, he set a course for Pteleum, the nearest point on the continent.

They had abundant proof from experience, after numerous embassies sent to Rome, and many conferences with Quinctius in person. This led them to conclude that nothing reasonable could ever be obtained from the Romans through negotiation. They would not consider seeking the aid of Antiochus until every last hope of such an outcome was eliminated. The king, with a thousand men, followed him from Demetrias. He took his route through Phocis. The Aetolian chiefs, going by another road, met at Cheronaea a small number of their young men whom they had called to arms. From there, in ten ships, they proceeded after him.

Antiochus pitched his camp at Salganea. He then led the Aetolian chiefs and himself across the Euripus in their ships. The people of Chalcis, therefore, stood not in need of an assertor of their liberty, as they already enjoyed it, nor did they require an armed protector, for they possessed both liberty and peace thanks to the kindness of the Roman people. They had no use for friendship, so the first instance they could offer was to leave the island and return home. As far as they were concerned, they were resolute in refusing to let them stay within their walls or even consider an alliance without Roman approval. " 47

He said that an immense body of cavalry was coming over the Hellespont into Europe. Some of them wore coats of mail, known as Cataphracti, while others fired arrows on horseback. What made it impossible to guard against them was that they could shoot with the surest aim even when their backs were turned and their horses were in full retreat. The Romans, therefore, had no need to contend with Philip or Hannibal. The former was a key figure in a commonwealth, while the latter was restricted to the kingdom of Macedonia alone. In contrast, they faced the mighty ruler of all Asia and parts of Europe. He was subsequently betrayed by his own intemperance into invective, sometimes directed at the Romans in general, and at times specifically against Quinctius. He accused them of ingratitude, scolding them for not only owing their victory over Philip but also their very survival; it was said that both Quinctius himself and his army had been saved by the Aetolians' efforts.

He would often encounter him in the field, taking oaths and making sacrifices, behaving like a mere augur; whereas he himself was putting his life at risk by exposing himself to the enemy's weapons. " 49 He had therefore been indifferent concerning the sentiments of the Achaeans, to whom he and his countrymen were conscious that they were thoroughly known. He studied to recommend himself to the king's ambassadors, and through them, to their absent master. This notion may be aptly applied to the forces of the king, which were so ostentatiously displayed in the past; that those various kinds of armor and numerous names of nations, previously unknown, are nothing more than Syrians, a race characterized by such base souls as make them better suited for slaves than soldiers.

The Boeotians made no definitive reply. They merely stated that when Antiochus arrived in Boeotia, they would then deliberate on the appropriate measures to be taken. When Antiochus heard that both the Achaeans and King Eumenes had sent reinforcements to Chalcis, he resolved to act with utmost expedition, so his troops could gain the upper hand and possibly intercept the others as they arrived; he then sent Menippus with about three thousand soldiers, and Polyxenidas with the entire fleet. He marched himself, leading six thousand of his own soldiers and a smaller number of Aetolians, gathered hastily from those at Lamia. The Roman soldiers, numbering around 500, arrived after Menippus had established his camp near Salganea. They came to Hermaeus, a strategic location that served as a crossing point between Boeotia and the island of Euboea.

In their opinion, the Aetolians had renounced friendship. This was due to their repeated demands for restitution, which they had made through their ambassadors. However, the Aetolians had not deemed it proper to provide either restitution or an apology. While these things were occurring at Rome, Antiochus, during the winter season in Chalcis, endeavoured to bring over several states through ambassadors sent among them; while many of their own accord sent deputies to him. The Epirots and the Eleans from Peloponnesus also followed suit. Antiochus himself led the expedition into Boeotia, citing the grievances against the Romans that I have already discussed: the death of Brachyllas and Quinctius's attack on Coronea over the massacre of Roman soldiers. However, the true reasons were that the nation's excellent policy, both public and private, had been in decline for several generations; many citizens found themselves unable to survive without a change in their circumstances.

A decree was passed in his favor, though veiled by a few words, against the Romans. After securing this nation on his side, he returned to Chalcis and dispatched letters, summoning the chief Aetolians to meet him at Demetrias. There, he would deliberate with them on the general plan of operations. He arrived with his ships on the day appointed for the council. Some people believed the attempt should be made right away, while others thought it would be better to wait until the spring season began.

Although the present debate turned chiefly on these points, Hannibal, being called on by name to give his opinion, led the king and those who were present into considering the general conduct of the war through a speech: "If I had been involved in your councils since we first entered Greece, when you were discussing Euboea, the Achaeans, and Boeotians, I would have offered the same advice I shall offer today, as your thoughts are focused on the Thessalians." It would be more advisable and advantageous to unite Philip with us than these. If he were to join our cause, he would have no room for retreat. He would bring a significant force that would not only strengthen our power in war against Rome but also demonstrate his ability to resist the Romans on his own. When you have assembled your entire force, divide the fleet. Keep one division at Corcyra to prevent the Romans from having a clear and safe passage. Send the other division to the coast of Italy opposite Sardinia and Africa. Meanwhile, with all your land forces, proceed to the territory of Bullium.

While he waited for Amynander and the Aetolians, he sent Philip, the Megalopolitan, with 2,000 men to collect the Macedonians' bones around Cynoscephalae, where the final battle had taken place against King Philip. He was advised to do this either to gain favor with the Macedonians and shift their displeasure onto the king for leaving his soldiers unburied or because he himself, driven by a desire for vain glory typical of kings, conceived such a plan. Although this step procured no thanks from the Macedonians, it excited Philip's heaviest displeasure. In consequence, he who had previously intended to regulate his counsels by the fortune of events now sent a message to Marcus Baebius, propraetor, immediately informing him that Antiochus had made an incursion into Thessaly. He advised Baebius to move out of his winter quarters if he wished, and that he himself would advance to meet Antiochus, so they could discuss what course of action was best to take together. " 9 While Antiochus was camped near Pherae, where the Aetolians and Amynander had joined him, ambassadors arrived from Larissa, seeking to understand the reasons behind his war against the Thessalians; they also requested that he withdraw his army. If necessary, they offered to discuss the matter with him through commissioners.

He offered remonstrances of a similar kind to those urged in behalf of the people of Chalcis at their first conference on the Euripus Strait. As the cases were similar, he urged some with greater boldness; the king requested that they consider his proposals seriously before adopting a resolution, which would provide immediate cause for regret if they were overly cautious and prudent about the future. They accordingly exerted their utmost efforts to defend their city. The king, on his part, resolved to assault the walls from all sides simultaneously. Considering that the fate of this city, the first he had besieged, would determine whether he should be feared or despised by the entire nation of Thessalians, he employed every possible means to instill terror in them everywhere. The king then lost no time; but while the alarm was still fresh, he sent 4,000 men against Scotussa, which surrendered without delay. Observing the recent example of those in Pherae, who, after suffering greatly, eventually compelled themselves to do what they had initially refused to do.

Within ten days of his arrival in Pherae, he led his entire army to Cranon, where they captured it upon his arrival. Meantime, Amynander, with the Athamanian troops, seized Pellinaeus. Menippus, with three thousand Aetolian foot and two hundred horse, marched into Perrhaebia, where he took Mallaea and Cyretiae by assault, and ravaged the lands of Tripolis. The king's advisors had completed their tasks and returned to Larissa just as the monarch was convening a council to discuss the best course of action regarding the location.

While others once represented the city's strength, surpassing even Pherae's in magnitude; at another time, the onset of winter rendered it unsuitable for military operations or besieging cities. The king's judgment hung in the balance between hope and fear, but his courage was bolstered by the arrival of ambassadors who happened to arrive just as news arrived from Pharsalus, announcing the city's surrender. Antiochus, therefore, pretending that the near approach of winter was his motive, stayed but one day longer, then withdrew from Larissa, and returned to Demetrius.

Although Appius realized that the siege's end meant his mission had been accomplished, he still decided to travel to Larissa to bolster the alliance's resolve for potential future challenges. The Larissans enjoyed a double happiness, for the enemy had left their country, and they also saw a Roman garrison in their city. 11 When he had won over her father, who was unwilling to bind himself with a condition in life involving such serious consequences, he first used messages and later personal entreaties to gain his consent. He then celebrated his wedding in the same way as if it were a time of great peace.

When the king arrived in Phocis at the beginning of spring, he convened a general assembly of all the troops there. He immediately noticed that the soldiers had spent the winter under discipline no stricter than that of their commander. Alexander ordered Alexander, an Acarnanian, and Menippus, a Macedonian, to lead his forces from there to Stratum in Aetolia. He then, having made offerings to Apollo at Delphi, went on to Naupactum. He attended a council with Aetolia's chiefs and then traveled along the route that passes through Chalcis and Lysimachia to Stratonikeia, where he would meet his army as it approached from the Malian Gulf.

The people of Leucas, in the city of Acarnania's capital, proved resistant to defection due to their fear of the approaching Roman fleets, led by Atilius, who was stationed near Cephallenia. He observed in the council that the inland parts of Acarnania should be guarded from danger. All able-bodied individuals were to march out to Medio and Thurium to prevent those places from being seized by Antiochus or the Aetolians. Some argued that only a body of five hundred men was necessary for this purpose, as not all the people needed to be called out in such a hasty manner. He had a sufficient number of soldiers at his disposal. He stationed 300 men in garrison at Medio and 200 at Thurium, intending for them to fall into the king's hands and serve as hostages thereafter. 12

Ambassadors from the king arrived in Medio. Upon hearing their proposal, the assembly began to discuss what response should be sent to the king. Some suggested maintaining the alliance with Rome, while others argued against rejecting the friendship of the king. Clitus proposed a middle ground, which was ultimately accepted as the compromise. Mnasilochus, along with some others from his faction, were included in this embassy. The king, sending private messengers to request that he bring up his army, deliberately wasted time. By the time the ambassadors had set out, Antiochus appeared in the territory and soon arrived at the city gates. Meanwhile, those who were not involved in the plot were in a state of panic and quickly called upon the young men to arms. Mnasilochus was escorted into the area by Clitus. As the season of the year strengthened, favourable for sailing, the credibility of this report was enhanced. The king, after placing a garrison in Medio and other towns of Acarnania, retired from Thurium and returned through the cities of Aetolia and Phocis to Chalcis. 13 About the same time, Marcus Baebius and King Philip reunited after a meeting in Dassaretia during the winter. Following their meeting, Appius Claudius was sent to Thessaly to lift the siege of Larissa. After returning to their winter quarters, they waited for the season to advance further before taking action. However, as spring began, they joined forces and marched into Thessaly.

He retired to Atrax. From there, he captured Cyretiae and Eritium, placing garrisons in the areas he had conquered. He then rejoined Philip, who was besieging Mallaea. The king would lead the assault on Limnaea, while Baebius remained to continue the siege of Pellinum. 14

Having been brought before the consul, he was ordered to be kept in confinement. Soon after, he was sent to Rome in chains. With his wife and children, he left the kingdom and retired to Ambracia. The consul delayed for a few days at Larissa, primarily to refresh his horses, which had been exhausted from the voyage and subsequent marching. After restoring some vigor to his army through moderate rest, he marched on to Cranon.

He then captured Proerna and the surrounding forts before beginning his march towards the Malian Bay. During these transactions, Antiochus was at Chalcis. He now perceived that he had gained nothing from Greece except winter quarters and a disgraceful marriage. He warmly blamed Thoas and the Aetolians for their fallacious promises. In contrast, he admired Hannibal, not only as a prudent man but also as one who foresaw all the events unfolding at that time. He decided to prevent his inconsiderate venture from being defeated by his own inaction. To this end, he ordered the Aetolians to arm all their able-bodied youth and gather them in a single force at Lamia.

He himself also immediately led them there, about 10,000 infantry and 500 cavalry. Thermopylae. The consul, after ravaging the lands of Hypata first and then those of Heraclea, where the Aetolian detachments proved useless in both places, encamped opposite the king at the entrance of the pass, near the warm springs. The Aetolians from both sides shut themselves up in Heraclea. He therefore dispatched a messenger to the Aetolians at Heraclea, requesting them to provide him with as much assistance as possible in the war, so that he could seize and secure the hilltops, thereby preventing the Romans from passing through them. When this message was received, a division arose among the Aetolians: some insisted that they should obey the king's orders and march, while others believed they should remain at Heraclea and wait for the outcome; for if the king were defeated by the consul, their forces would be fresh and ready to provide aid to their own states in the vicinity. If he were victorious, they could pursue the Romans as they fled in disarray.

But this man was one who, apart from his former life, had accomplished nothing remarkable after coming to Europe from Asia to wage war on the Roman people. During the entire winter, he had taken a wife for passion's sake from a private house, which was even obscure among its neighbors. Now, as a newly married man, surfeited with nuptial feasts, he emerged to fight. They were asked to defend both Hypata and Heraclea, but they did neither. Instead, half of them retreated to the mountain peaks, while the rest barricaded themselves inside Heraclea. The king himself admitted that, far from daring to meet us in battle on the level plain, he was too afraid to even set up camp in open ground. He had abandoned all the land in front of him, which he had boasted about taking from us and Philip, and was hiding behind the rocks.

He dismissed the soldiers after this discourse, who prepared their armor and weapons before eating. Afterwards, the enemy pressed on with greater force, and they were forced to yield. Filing off from the rear, they retreated within the fortifications. By extending their spears before them, they formed a second line of defense. The original rampart was not high enough to provide a significant advantage, but its defenders could use their spears to keep the enemy at bay beneath it.

Many people approached the work inconsiderately. Some were run through the body and either abandoned their attempt or lost a great number of men. This was due to Marcus Porcius arriving from the summit of Callidromus, where he had dislodged the Aetolians after killing most of them. The city of Heraclea was attacked by the Aetolians while it was occupied as a garrison. Despite the boldness shown by those involved in the action, the endeavor ultimately proved unsuccessful. As the consul led his army through Phocis and Boeotia, the revolted states, aware of their defection, presented themselves at the gates of their cities, bearing tokens of supplication. The army proceeded, treating them as if they were in a friendly country, without offering any violence, until it reached the territory of Coronea.

The soldiers were therefore immediately recalled, and the depredations ceased. The rest of the cities in Euboea also submitted without opposition. Once peace was restored throughout the island within a few days, no punishment was inflicted on any city. The army, which had gained more praise for its moderation after the victory than for the victory itself, was led back to Thermopylae. They carried on this practice in several small groups, with great enthusiasm at first, but later in fewer numbers and with less energy.

For though they had a multiplicity of difficulties to struggle with, what above all things utterly consumed their vigour was the lack of sleep. The Romans, having plenty of men, relieved each other regularly in their posts. In contrast, among the Aetolians, whose numbers were small, the same persons were constantly exhausted by unremitting labour day and night. For twenty-four days, without a single moment of respite, they continued their labor against the enemy's relentless attacks, which were launched from four distinct directions simultaneously. As soon as the signal of retreat was given to the Romans, they all gladly retired from their posts. They did not reappear on the walls until after the third hour of the day. 24

The Romans attempted to breach the walls by climbing over the rubble through the gaps, while others used ladders to ascend. The Aetolians rushed to defend the areas under attack from all sides. As soon as the Aetolians heard the shout, they abandoned their posts and fled everywhere towards the citadel. The victors sacked the city. The consul had granted permission, not to satisfy a desire for revenge or hostility, but rather to allow the soldiers, who had been restrained from plundering in so many cities they had captured from the enemy, to finally experience the rewards of their victory.

They laid down their arms and submitted. Philip, by concert, besieged Lamia. He was not mistaken in his opinion. As soon as Heraclea fell, he received a message urging him to withdraw from the attack. It was more logical that the Roman soldiers, who had fought the Aetolians on the battlefield, would receive the reward for their victory. Thus was Lamia relieved. The misfortune of a neighboring city proved the means of her escape from a similar disaster. 26

They were to remind him that "it concerned his dignity and his honour, not to abandon his allies; and it likewise concerned the safety of his kingdom, not to leave the Romans at full leisure after ruining the nation of the Aetolians to carry their whole force into Asia." What they said was true, and therefore made a deeper impression on the king. In consequence, he immediately supplied the ambassadors with the necessary funds for the war's exigencies and assured them that he would send them both troops and ships. The loss of Heraclea entirely broke the spirits of the Aetolians; within a few days after sending ambassadors into Asia to renew the war and invite the king, they abandoned all warlike designs and dispatched deputies to the consul to negotiate a peace. When the consul began to speak, interrupting them, he said he had other business to attend to at present. He ordered them to return to Hypata and granted them a truce for ten days. With this, he sent Lucius Valerius Flaccus along with them, instructing that any business they intended to discuss should be communicated to him as well as any other matters they deemed proper.

In that case, the latter would be ashamed to do injury to suppliants. Meanwhile, they would still possess the ability to consider their own interests, provided that fortune presented them with something even more beneficial. " 28 Phaeneas then said, "As for myself and my fellow countrymen present, we knew that his commands must be obeyed. However, it was necessary for a council of the Aetolians to meet to pass decrees accordingly. For this purpose, he requested a suspension of arms for ten days." At the request of Flaccus on behalf of the Aetolians, this was granted, and they returned to Hypata. When Phaeneas spoke here, in the select council called Apocleti, he recounted the orders they had received and the treatment they had narrowly escaped. Although the chiefs lamented their condition, they were of the opinion that the conqueror must be obeyed, and that the Aetolians should be summoned from all their towns to a general assembly. 29

This man, after completing his embassy's business, returned to Aetolia. On the twelfth day following his departure, he arrived at Phalara, situated on the Malian Bay. Philip, upon learning of his arrival, welcomed him as a guest rather than an enemy. He invited him to sit down and join in the festivities. Later, after dismissing everyone else, Philip kept him alone and reassured him that he had nothing to fear for himself. He severely censured the conduct of the Aetolians for bringing, first the Romans, and then Antiochus, into Greece. Their designs were motivated by a lack of judgment, which ultimately backfired on themselves.

"He would forget all past transactions, which it was easier to blame than to amend. He would not act in such a way as to appear to insult their misfortunes." It would be fitting for the Aetolians to put aside their animosity toward him. In turn, Nicander, in his personal capacity, would come to recall that particular day when he was saved by him. Nicander had appointed people to escort him to a safe location. Upon arrival, he reached Hypata, where his fellow citizens were discussing a peace treaty with Rome. 30 Manius Acilius, having sold or given the spoils found near Heraclea to the soldiers, learned that the counsels adopted at Hypata were not of a peaceful nature but rather that the Aetolians had hastily assembled at Naupactum with the intention of making a stand there against the entire force of the war. He then sent Appius Claudius forward with 4,000 men to seize the mountain passes, which were difficult to navigate. Appius Claudius himself ascended Mount Oeta and offered sacrifices to Hercules at Pyra,[1] a site where the mortal part of the demi-god was said to have been burned.

The Achaeans laid siege to Messene in Peloponnese, as it refused to join their league. The two city-states of Messene and Elis were not affiliated with the Achaean Confederacy and instead aligned themselves with the Aetolians. The Eleans, following Antiochus' departure from Greece, responded to the Achaean deputies with greater moderation. They stated that once the king's troops had been removed, they would assess which role they should assume. The Messenians had dismissed the deputies without a reply, and prepared for war. Alarmed by their own situation, they saw the enemy ravaging their country without control, and pitching their camp close to their city. They sent deputies to Chalcis, where Titus Quinctius, the author of their liberty, resided, to inform him that "the Messenians were willing to open their gates and surrender their city to the Romans, but not to the Achaeans." Quinctius heard this and immediately set out. He dispatched a messenger from Megalopolis to Diophanes, the Achaean praetor, ordering him to withdraw his army at once from Messene and come to him.

Amynander gave the government of the island to Philip, the Megalopolitan. Later, during the war in which he allied himself with Antiochus against the Romans, Amynander summoned Philip to fulfill his campaign duties. As his successor, Hierocles, from Agrigentum, was appointed. This man, after Antiochus' flight from Thermopylae and Philip's expulsion of Amynander from Athamania, sent emissaries at his own expense to Diophanes, praetor of the Achaeans. He then bargained for a sum of money and delivered over the island to the Achaeans. The Romans claimed this acquisition, made during the war, as their own. They stated that it was not for Diophanes and the Achaean forces that Consul Manius Acilius and the Roman legions fought at Thermopylae. Diophanes, in answer, sometimes apologized for himself and his nation; sometimes insisted on the justice of the proceedings.

Laying aside all sternness in his voice and demeanor, he said: "If, O Achaean people, I thought the possession of that island advantageous to you, I would be the first to advise the senate and people of Rome to permit you to hold it." There was a large number of the king's soldiers in the place. A few had been left behind as a garrison at first, but most of the others had fled there after the defeat of his army. They were mostly unarmed and lacked both the physical strength and moral courage needed to withstand a siege. While Philip was thus employed, Titus Quinctius, after receiving from the Achaean council the cession of Zacynthus, crossed over to Naupactum. The city had endured a nearly two-month siege but was now in a desperate state. It was believed that if it fell, the entire nation of the Aetolians would be destroyed.

Although he was incensed against the Aetolians, he remembered that they alone had attempted to depreciate his merits when he granted Greece its freedom. They had also been unaffected by his advice when he warned them of the events that would soon unfold, in an effort to deter them from their ill-fated endeavor. Nonetheless, considering it his duty to ensure that none of the liberated Greek states fell under his own leadership were subverted, he began walking near the city walls, so he could be easily identified by the Aetolians. He was deeply moved by their requests, but at that moment, he signaled with his hands that they should not expect any help from him. "He then said, 'Your condition causes me to restrain my resentment and refrain from giving you reproof.'

Neither the Achaeans nor the Eleans carried out this plan, for they chose to reserve the credit for achieving it themselves. The Eleans, however, preferred to join the Achaean alliance voluntarily, rather than through Roman mediation. Although they had not provided Antiochus with any soldiers, they were nonetheless accused of having aided him financially. They neither denied nor confirmed that they had sent envoys to him. He would therefore take no step in the business, but leave it to be determined at Rome. For this purpose, he granted them a truce of ninety days. When the Epirots, who were sent to Rome, addressed the senate, they rather enumerated hostile acts they had not committed than cleared themselves of those laid to their charge. They received such an answer that they seemed rather to have obtained pardon than proved their innocence.

His demand was deemed unprecedented and unreasonable. They therefore voted that whatever games he had vowed to celebrate alone, without consulting the senate, would be done from the spoils, provided he had set aside anything for the purpose; otherwise, he would have to bear the cost himself. Publius Cornelius exhibited those games for ten days. About this time, the temple of the Great Idaean Mother was dedicated. The deity, brought from Asia during the consulate of Publius Cornelius Scipio, later known as Africanus, and Publius Lucinius, who had previously led the procession from the sea-side to the Palatine. At the beginning of the year in which those transactions took place, after Manius Acilius had departed to open the campaign, and while the other consul, Publius Cornelius, remained in Rome, two tame oxen, it is said, climbed up by ladders on the tiles of a house in the Carina.

About two months later, Consul Publius Cornelius engaged in a fierce battle with the Boian army, achieving remarkable success. Although we may not entirely credit this writer with respect to the numbers, as in such exaggeration no writer is more extravagant, yet it is certain that the victory on this occasion was very complete. The enemy's camp was taken immediately after the battle, and the Boians surrendered themselves. A supplication was also decreed by the senate on account of this victory, and victims of greater kinds were sacrificed. Publius Cornelius, the consul, received hostages from the Boians. He punished them by appropriating nearly half of their land for the benefit of the Roman people. The Boians could then choose to send colonies there in the future.

The next day, after his arrival, he convened a meeting of the senate in the temple of Bellona. There, he outlined the services he had rendered and requested permission to ride through the city in triumph. The Wars of the Ligurians were always united with the Wars of the Gauls. These two nations, located near each other, provided mutual assistance to one another. When the Ligurians were subdued, Publius Cornelius, as proconsul, might triumph. A few months later, he would triumph, following in the footsteps of many predecessors who had not attained this honor until the end of their term. " 40

He confidently hoped that, in a short time, Quintus Minucius would demand and obtain a well-deserved triumph. He demanded a triumph over the Boian Gauls, whom he had conquered in battle and driven out of their camp. Within two days of the fight, his entire nation submitted unconditionally. He also brought back hostages to ensure lasting peace. He summoned an assembly the next day and explained how he had been unfairly treated by the tribune who sought to entangle him in matters beyond his authority. The tribune's intention was to deprive him of the rewards he had earned. In response, he released the soldiers from their oath and dismissed them. 41

While Antiochus was in Italy, he was at Ephesus, free from all concern about the war with Rome, as he believed the Romans had no intention of entering Asia. This state of security was caused by the false opinions or flattery of most of his friends. He therefore advised Antiochus not to harbor vain hopes for peace. The king, therefore, set sail for Chersonesus with his fully prepared fleet, intending to fortify the region with garrisons, in case the Romans were to arrive by land.

Samos and Zacynthus had been ravaged by Sam's passage, as they favored the Aetolian party. He altered his course for Malea and met favorable weather, arriving in a few days at Piraeus, where he joined the old fleet. The king, upon hearing that the Roman fleet was approaching, withdrew to Magnesia, near Sipylus, to collect his land forces. His ships proceeded to Cyssus, a port of Erythraea, where they awaited the enemy's arrival with greater convenience. They subsequently brought their fleet around to the city of Chios, where they took on supplies before sailing to Phocaea. Eumenes, who had gone to join his fleet at Elaea, returned a few days later, bringing twenty-four ships with crews, to Phocaea, where the Romans were preparing for battle.

The ships were thus compelled to sail individually, one after another, in a narrow line. Once the storm subsided, they attempted to make their way towards the harbour of Corycus, located beyond Cyssus. 44 All their ships were now in sight. Two Carthaginian vessels, which had advanced ahead of the Romans, encountered three belonging to the king. Eumenes, who arrived last, launched his attack immediately after the battle began. Seeing the left squadron of the enemy in disarray caused by Livius, he targeted their right flank, where the fight was still evenly matched. 45

In a short time, a flight commenced. Initially, it was led by the left squadron, with Polyxenidas taking command. He realized he was outmatched in terms of bravery among his men and decided to retreat by hoisting his top-sails. Shortly after, those who had engaged with Eumenes near land also chose to flee. The Romans stayed, during the remainder of that day, in the port from which the king's fleet had sailed out. On the day following, they proceeded in the pursuit. They sailed over to Phocaea after a few days.
